name: Isoniazid

dosage_guidelines.adults: 300 mg once daily

warnings: Monitor liver enzymes regularly
Take with pyridoxine (Vitamin B6) to prevent nerve damage
Avoid alcohol during treatment

name: Rifampicin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 600 mg once daily (empty stomach)

warnings: Turns body fluids orange-red (harmless)
Potent enzyme inducer — interacts with many drugs
Monitor liver function throughout treatment

name: Pyrazinamide

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1500–2000 mg once daily (weight-based)

warnings: Can raise uric acid levels — monitor in gout-prone patients
Hepatotoxic — avoid in severe liver disease
Used only in intensive phase (2 months)

name: Ethambutol

dosage_guidelines.adults: 15–25 mg/kg once daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: First 2 months (or full 6 months in some regimens)

warnings: Regular eye tests required — can cause irreversible vision loss
Avoid in young children who cannot report visual changes
Reduce dose in renal impairment

name: Paracetamol (Acetaminophen)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500–1000 mg every 4–6 hours (max 4 g/day)

warnings: Do NOT use NSAIDs or aspirin in dengue — increases bleeding risk
Do not exceed 4 g/day in adults
Avoid alcohol during use

name: Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS)

dosage_guidelines.adults: As much as needed to maintain hydration (500 mL–1 L per loose stool)
dosage_guidelines.children: 50–100 mL/kg over 2–4 hours for mild dehydration

warnings: Do not add extra sugar or salt to ORS packets
Seek IV fluids if vomiting persists and oral intake is impossible
Monitor urine output as a hydration marker

name: Chloroquine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 600 mg base initially, then 300 mg at 6, 24, 48 hours
dosage_guidelines.children: 10 mg/kg initially, then 5 mg/kg at 6, 24, 48 hours

warnings: Only effective against P. vivax and P. ovale — resistance common in P. falciparum
Avoid in patients with G6PD deficiency
Long-term use can damage the retina

name: Artemether-Lumefantrine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 80/480 mg twice daily for 3 days (with food)
dosage_guidelines.children: Weight-based dosing: 5–<15 kg: 20/120 mg twice daily for 3 days

warnings: Must be taken with fatty food for adequate absorption
Do not use in first trimester of pregnancy
Monitor for cardiac arrhythmia in at-risk patients

name: Primaquine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 15 mg base once daily for 14 days (P. vivax)

warnings: Test for G6PD deficiency before use — can cause life-threatening hemolysis
Contraindicated in pregnancy
Do not use in infants under 6 months

name: Naproxen

dosage_guidelines.adults: 250–500 mg twice daily with food

warnings: Avoid in dengue co-infection — bleeding risk
Use with caution in peptic ulcer disease
Can increase cardiovascular risk with long-term use

name: Chlorpheniramine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 4 mg every 4–6 hours (max 24 mg/day)

warnings: Causes significant drowsiness — avoid driving
Avoid in prostate enlargement or glaucoma
Use cautiously in elderly (risk of falls)

name: Ciprofloxacin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg twice daily

warnings: Increasing resistance to fluoroquinolones in South Asia — check sensitivity
Avoid excessive sun exposure
Risk of tendon rupture, especially in elderly

name: Azithromycin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg once daily

warnings: Preferred for fluoroquinolone-resistant typhoid
Monitor cardiac rhythm in susceptible patients
Avoid in patients with liver disease

name: Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 L ORS per loose stool; IV fluids if severe
dosage_guidelines.children: 75 mL/kg over 4 hours for moderate dehydration

warnings: IV fluid (Ringer's Lactate) is essential in severe cholera — ORS alone is insufficient
Monitor electrolytes in severe cases
Do not add extra salt to ORS — use standard sachet formulation

name: Doxycycline

dosage_guidelines.adults: 300 mg single dose OR 100 mg twice daily for 3 days

warnings: Do not use in children under 8 years or pregnant women
Take with plenty of water — sit upright for 30 min after
Avoid sun exposure — use sunscreen

name: Hepatitis A Vaccine
symptoms_treated: prevents fatigue and fever from HAV

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 mL IM (deltoid); booster at 6–12 months
dosage_guidelines.children: 0.5 mL IM; 2 doses 6–18 months apart (from age 1)

warnings: Not therapeutic — preventive only
Administer before potential exposure in endemic areas
Defer in severely ill patients

name: Tenofovir Disoproxil Fumarate (TDF)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 300 mg once daily with or without food

warnings: Monitor renal function periodically
Severe flare of hepatitis B may occur if drug is stopped abruptly
Adjust dose in renal impairment

name: Entecavir

dosage_guidelines.adults: 0.5 mg once daily (1 mg in lamivudine-resistant)

warnings: Do not stop abruptly — risk of severe hepatitis flare
Monitor for lactic acidosis symptoms (weakness, abdominal pain)
Adjust dose in renal insufficiency

name: Sofosbuvir + Velpatasvir

dosage_guidelines.adults: 400/100 mg once daily with food
dosage_guidelines.children: Not approved under 18 for this combination

warnings: Do not use with amiodarone — risk of serious bradycardia
Test for HBV before starting — HBV reactivation risk
Monitor for drug interactions (rifampicin reduces efficacy)

name: Sofosbuvir + Ledipasvir

dosage_guidelines.adults: 400/90 mg once daily
dosage_guidelines.children: 17 kg and above: 200/45 mg once daily

warnings: Check for drug interactions with antacids and acid-reducing agents (take 4 hours apart)
Monitor renal function in patients on TDF combination
Screen for HBV co-infection before initiating

name: Oseltamivir (Tamiflu)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 75 mg twice daily
dosage_guidelines.children: Weight-based: <15 kg: 30 mg twice daily; 15–23 kg: 45 mg twice daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: 5 days (start within 48 hours of symptom onset)

warnings: Most effective when started within 48 hours of symptoms
Not a substitute for flu vaccination
Monitor for unusual behavior in children and adolescents

name: Zanamivir (Relenza)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10 mg inhaled twice daily (2 inhalations of 5 mg)
dosage_guidelines.children: Same as adults (≥7 years for treatment)

warnings: Contraindicated in asthma or COPD — risk of severe bronchospasm
Inhaled route — requires correct inhaler technique
Keep bronchodilator available when using in respiratory disease

name: Nirmatrelvir + Ritonavir (Paxlovid)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Nirmatrelvir 300 mg + Ritonavir 100 mg twice daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: 5 days (start within 5 days of symptom onset)

warnings: Significant drug interactions via CYP3A4 — review all medications
Not for use in severe hepatic impairment
COVID rebound possible after completing course

name: Dexamethasone

dosage_guidelines.adults: 6 mg once daily (oral or IV)

warnings: Only beneficial in patients requiring oxygen or ventilation — harmful in mild COVID
Monitor blood sugar, especially in diabetics
Do not stop abruptly after prolonged use

name: Remdesivir

dosage_guidelines.adults: 200 mg IV on day 1, then 100 mg IV daily

warnings: IV administration only (hospital setting)
Monitor liver function and renal function during therapy
Infuse slowly — rapid infusion causes bradycardia

name: Vitamin A

dosage_guidelines.adults: 200,000 IU once daily for 2 days
dosage_guidelines.children: < 6 months: 50,000 IU; 6–11 months: 100,000 IU; ≥12 months: 200,000 IU for 2 days

warnings: WHO-recommended for all children with measles in developing countries
Do not repeat within 1 month — risk of vitamin A toxicity
Seek medical attention for measles complications (pneumonia, encephalitis)

name: Ibuprofen

dosage_guidelines.adults: 400 mg every 6–8 hours with food

warnings: Avoid in renal impairment or dehydration
Take with food to reduce GI side effects
Avoid in patients with peptic ulcer disease

name: Paracetamol (Acetaminophen)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500–1000 mg every 4–6 hours (max 4 g/day)

warnings: Do not exceed 4 g/day
Avoid in liver disease
Rubella is most dangerous in early pregnancy — vaccinate before conception

name: Acyclovir

dosage_guidelines.adults: 800 mg 5 times daily

warnings: Most effective when started within 24 hours of rash onset
Maintain adequate hydration to protect kidneys
Adjust dose in renal impairment

name: Calamine Lotion

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply to affected area 3–4 times daily
dosage_guidelines.children: Apply gently 3–4 times daily (avoid near eyes)

warnings: For external use only — do not apply near eyes or mucous membranes
Discontinue if skin irritation worsens
Not a substitute for antiviral treatment in severe cases

name: Dapsone

dosage_guidelines.adults: 100 mg once daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: 12 months (MB leprosy); 6 months (PB leprosy) — in MDT combination

warnings: Test for G6PD deficiency before use
Always used in WHO Multi-Drug Therapy (MDT) — never monotherapy
Monitor CBC regularly for anemia

name: Rifampicin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 600 mg once monthly (supervised)

warnings: Taken as monthly supervised dose in leprosy MDT
Potent drug interaction — check all concurrent medications
Liver function monitoring required

name: Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 20 IU/kg infiltrated into wound site; remainder IM
dosage_guidelines.duration: Single dose at time of first vaccine dose

warnings: Must be given with first dose of rabies vaccine — never alone
Infiltrate as much as possible into wound; remainder given IM at distant site
Do not administer in same syringe or site as vaccine

name: Rabies Vaccine (HDCV/PCECV)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 mL IM (deltoid) on days 0, 3, 7, 14 (post-exposure)
dosage_guidelines.children: Same dosing as adults (anterolateral thigh in young children)

warnings: Never delay — rabies is 100% fatal once symptomatic
Inject into deltoid (not gluteal — poor immune response)
Pre-exposure prophylaxis available for high-risk individuals

name: Amphotericin B (Liposomal)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 3–5 mg/kg/day IV for 3–5 days (single course)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Total dose 15–20 mg/kg over treatment course

warnings: Monitor renal function and electrolytes throughout treatment
Pre-medicate with paracetamol and antihistamine to reduce infusion reactions
Liposomal form preferred over conventional due to lower toxicity

name: Diethylcarbamazine (DEC)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 6 mg/kg/day in 3 divided doses
dosage_guidelines.duration: 12 days (standard course); mass drug administration: single annual dose

side_effects: allergic reactions from dying worms (Mazzotti reaction)
warnings: Allergic reactions are due to dying microfilariae — not the drug itself
Contraindicated in onchocerciasis co-endemic areas
Not to be used during acute filarial fever or adenolymphangitis

name: Tenofovir + Lamivudine + Dolutegravir (TLD)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 300/300/50 mg once daily (fixed-dose combination)

warnings: Never stop ART without medical guidance — viral rebound and resistance
Screen for HBV before starting (TDF covers both HBV and HIV)
Monitor for neuropsychiatric symptoms with dolutegravir

name: Cotrimoxazole (Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 960 mg once daily (prophylaxis)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Until CD4 > 200 cells/µL on ART for ≥6 months

warnings: Contraindicated in sulfa allergy
Monitor CBC and renal function
Folate supplementation recommended with long-term use

name: Zinc Sulfate

dosage_guidelines.adults: Not routinely used in adults

warnings: Give with small amount of food or breastmilk to reduce nausea
Do not use adult zinc supplements in children
WHO recommends zinc with ORS for all childhood diarrhea

name: Amoxicillin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg every 8 hours OR 875 mg every 12 hours
dosage_guidelines.children: 40–90 mg/kg/day divided (high dose for severe cases)

warnings: First-line for community-acquired pneumonia in outpatient settings
Hospitalized patients may need IV antibiotics
Reassess if no improvement within 48–72 hours

name: Azithromycin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg on day 1, then 250 mg daily

warnings: Covers atypical organisms (Mycoplasma, Legionella)
Risk of cardiac arrhythmia — avoid in patients with QT prolongation
Often combined with beta-lactam for community-acquired pneumonia

name: Metformin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg twice daily with meals; max 2500 mg/day

warnings: Hold before contrast dye procedures or surgery
Contraindicated in severe renal impairment (eGFR < 30)
Risk of lactic acidosis with heavy alcohol use

name: Insulin Glargine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10 units SC once daily at bedtime (titrate based on fasting glucose)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Lifelong for type 1; long-term for type 2

warnings: Never mix with other insulins in same syringe
Monitor blood glucose regularly — dose adjustment needed
Rotate injection sites to prevent lipodystrophy

name: Empagliflozin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10 mg once daily; may increase to 25 mg

warnings: Hold before surgery — risk of ketoacidosis
Ensure adequate hydration
Avoid in recurrent UTI or genital infections

name: Amlodipine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 5 mg once daily; may increase to 10 mg

warnings: Ankle edema is common and dose-dependent
Do not stop abruptly — rebound hypertension risk
Grapefruit juice can increase drug levels

name: Enalapril

dosage_guidelines.adults: 5–40 mg once or twice daily

warnings: Contraindicated in pregnancy — causes fetal harm
Risk of angioedema (facial swelling) — seek emergency care if occurs
Monitor potassium and renal function regularly

name: Losartan

dosage_guidelines.adults: 50–100 mg once daily

warnings: Contraindicated in pregnancy
Do not combine with ACE inhibitor (dual blockade risk)
Monitor renal function and potassium

name: Aspirin (Low Dose)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 75–100 mg once daily (long-term); 300 mg loading dose in acute MI
dosage_guidelines.children: Not routinely used in children for CAD

warnings: Avoid on empty stomach — take with food or antacid
Do not use if active peptic ulcer or GI bleeding
Avoid in children with viral illness — Reye's syndrome risk

name: Atorvastatin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10–80 mg once daily at night

warnings: Report unexplained muscle pain — risk of rhabdomyolysis
Monitor liver enzymes periodically
Avoid with certain antibiotics (clarithromycin) — increases statin toxicity

name: Clopidogrel

dosage_guidelines.adults: 75 mg once daily; 300–600 mg loading dose in ACS
dosage_guidelines.duration: 12 months post-stent; lifelong in some cases

warnings: Do not stop abruptly after coronary stent — fatal stent thrombosis risk
Omeprazole/esomeprazole reduce its effectiveness (use pantoprazole instead)
Bleeding risk increases when combined with aspirin or NSAIDs

name: Alteplase (tPA)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 0.9 mg/kg IV (max 90 mg); 10% as bolus, rest over 60 min
dosage_guidelines.duration: Single dose within 4.5 hours of stroke onset

warnings: Must be given within 4.5 hours of symptom onset — time is brain
Contraindicated if recent surgery, bleeding disorder, or hemorrhagic stroke
Hospital administration only under stroke specialist care

name: Salbutamol (Albuterol)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 100–200 mcg inhaled (MDI) as needed; 2.5 mg nebulized
dosage_guidelines.children: 100 mcg per puff; 1–2 puffs as needed

warnings: Not for long-term regular use alone in COPD — use with maintenance inhaler
Overuse indicates poor disease control — reassess therapy
Monitor heart rate in elderly patients

name: Tiotropium

dosage_guidelines.adults: 18 mcg inhaled once daily (HandiHaler) or 5 mcg (Respimat)

warnings: Avoid in narrow-angle glaucoma — can precipitate acute attack
Use with caution in prostate enlargement
Do not swallow capsule — for inhalation only

name: Budesonide + Formoterol (Symbicort)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 160/4.5 mcg or 320/9 mcg; 1–2 puffs twice daily

warnings: Rinse mouth after each use — prevents oral thrush
Do not use formoterol alone without ICS in asthma
Not a rescue inhaler — use salbutamol for acute attacks

name: Montelukast

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10 mg once daily at bedtime
dosage_guidelines.children: 6–14 years: 5 mg; 2–5 years: 4 mg once daily

warnings: FDA black box warning for neuropsychiatric effects — depression, suicidal thoughts
Not for acute attacks — use bronchodilator
Discuss risks vs. benefits especially in patients with depression or anxiety

name: Ondansetron

dosage_guidelines.adults: 8 mg IV/orally before chemotherapy; then 8 mg every 8–12 hours

warnings: Risk of QT prolongation — avoid with other QT-prolonging drugs
Constipation may be severe — use stool softener prophylactically
IV formulation can cause transient vision changes

name: Erythropoietin (EPO)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 50–100 units/kg SC 3 times/week (dialysis patients)

warnings: Do not target Hb > 11–12 g/dL — increases cardiovascular risk
Ensure iron stores are adequate before use
Monitor blood pressure closely — can worsen hypertension

name: Spironolactone

dosage_guidelines.adults: 100–400 mg once daily

warnings: Monitor potassium — hyperkalemia can be fatal
Avoid NSAIDs — worsen ascites and kidney function
Gynecomastia is dose-dependent and may be irreversible

name: Orlistat

dosage_guidelines.adults: 120 mg three times daily with each main meal
dosage_guidelines.duration: Up to 2 years (alongside diet and exercise)

warnings: Take a multivitamin 2 hours before or after Orlistat — blocks fat-soluble vitamins
Adhere to low-fat diet — high fat meal causes severe GI side effects
Monitor thyroid hormone levels in patients on levothyroxine

name: Celecoxib

dosage_guidelines.adults: 200 mg once daily or 100 mg twice daily

warnings: Increased cardiovascular risk with long-term use — use lowest effective dose
Avoid in sulfa allergy
Do not use in heart failure or after recent bypass surgery

name: Methotrexate

dosage_guidelines.adults: 7.5–25 mg once weekly (oral or SC)

warnings: Take folic acid 5 mg weekly (not same day as MTX) to reduce side effects
Contraindicated in pregnancy — causes fetal death
Monitor CBC and liver function every 4–8 weeks

name: Hydroxychloroquine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 200–400 mg once daily (max 5 mg/kg/day)

warnings: Annual ophthalmology eye exam required — can cause irreversible retinal damage
Avoid in G6PD deficiency
Reduces dose in renal impairment

name: Alendronate

dosage_guidelines.adults: 70 mg once weekly OR 10 mg once daily

warnings: Take on empty stomach with 8 oz water; stay upright for 30 minutes
Do not take with food, coffee, or other medications
Report jaw pain or swelling — rare osteonecrosis risk

name: Levothyroxine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1.6 mcg/kg/day once daily (titrate to TSH)

warnings: Take 30–60 min before breakfast on empty stomach for best absorption
Calcium, iron, and antacids reduce absorption — separate by 4 hours
Over-replacement increases risk of atrial fibrillation and bone loss

name: Carbimazole

dosage_guidelines.adults: 20–60 mg/day in divided doses initially; maintenance 5–15 mg/day

warnings: Report sore throat or fever immediately — may signal agranulocytosis
Avoid in first trimester pregnancy (use PTU instead)
Monitor thyroid function and CBC regularly

name: Sertraline

dosage_guidelines.adults: 50 mg once daily; may increase to 200 mg
dosage_guidelines.duration: Minimum 6 months; 2+ years for recurrent depression

warnings: Increased suicidal ideation in children and young adults — monitor closely
Do not stop abruptly — taper slowly to avoid discontinuation syndrome
Risk of serotonin syndrome with other serotonergic drugs (tramadol, linezolid)

name: Escitalopram

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10 mg once daily; may increase to 20 mg

warnings: Do not stop abruptly — taper over several weeks
Risk of hyponatremia (low sodium) in elderly
Avoid with MAOIs — risk of serotonin syndrome

name: Donepezil

dosage_guidelines.adults: 5 mg once daily at bedtime; increase to 10 mg after 4–6 weeks
dosage_guidelines.duration: Long-term; benefits last as long as drug is taken

warnings: May worsen syncope or bradycardia in patients with cardiac conduction issues
Cholinergic side effects worse at higher doses (10 mg)
Does not cure or stop progression — symptomatic benefit only

name: Levodopa + Carbidopa

dosage_guidelines.adults: Start 25/100 mg 3 times daily; titrate every 3–7 days

warnings: Take 30 min before meals for best absorption (high-protein meals reduce absorption)
Hallucinations more common in elderly — reduce dose
"On-off" fluctuations develop over time — specialist management needed

name: Ferrous Sulfate

dosage_guidelines.adults: 200 mg (65 mg elemental iron) 2–3 times daily
dosage_guidelines.children: 3–6 mg/kg/day of elemental iron in divided doses
dosage_guidelines.duration: 3–6 months to replenish stores after Hb normalizes

warnings: Take on empty stomach for best absorption but with food if intolerable
Keep out of reach of children — iron overdose is life-threatening
Take 2 hours apart from calcium, antacids, or tetracycline

name: Vitamin A Capsule

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10,000 IU/day for deficiency; 200,000 IU once for severe cases
dosage_guidelines.children: < 6 months: 50,000 IU; 6–11 months: 100,000 IU; ≥12 months: 200,000 IU
dosage_guidelines.duration: Single high-dose or 1–2 weeks for correction

warnings: Do not exceed recommended dose — Vitamin A is toxic in excess
Avoid high doses in pregnancy — teratogenic (causes birth defects)
WHO recommends supplementation for children < 5 years in deficiency areas

name: Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 60,000 IU once weekly for 8 weeks, then 1,000–2,000 IU/day maintenance
dosage_guidelines.children: 1,000–2,000 IU/day or 60,000 IU monthly (age and weight dependent)

warnings: High doses over time can cause toxicity — monitor calcium levels
Take with fatty meal for better absorption
25-OH Vitamin D levels should guide therapy duration

name: Potassium Iodide / Iodized Salt

dosage_guidelines.adults: 150 mcg iodine/day (dietary)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Lifelong via iodized salt; therapeutic dosing under physician guidance

side_effects: iodism (metallic taste, runny nose at high doses)
warnings: Excess iodine can trigger hyperthyroidism in susceptible individuals
Best addressed via universal iodization of salt at population level
Avoid high-dose supplements unless medically directed

name: Therapeutic Food (RUTF — Ready-to-Use Therapeutic Food)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Not primarily used in adults
dosage_guidelines.children: 175–220 kcal/kg/day based on weight; 92 g packet per day for ~6 kg child

warnings: Monitor for refeeding syndrome in severely malnourished children — reintroduce calories slowly
Always start with F-75 (low-calorie therapeutic milk) before RUTF in inpatient settings
Ensure adequate water intake alongside RUTF

name: Ampicillin + Gentamicin

dosage_guidelines.adults: Not applicable
dosage_guidelines.children: Ampicillin 50 mg/kg IV every 12 hrs (0–7 days); Gentamicin 4 mg/kg IV once daily

warnings: Monitor gentamicin levels and renal function
Hearing loss risk — minimize gentamicin duration
Adjust dose based on gestational and postnatal age

name: Caffeine Citrate

dosage_guidelines.adults: Not applicable
dosage_guidelines.children: 20 mg/kg IV loading dose; 5–10 mg/kg/day maintenance (preterm neonates)

warnings: Monitor heart rate — excessive doses cause tachycardia
Use with caution in neonates with cardiac arrhythmia
Neonatal metabolism varies greatly — adjust based on response

name: Amoxicillin

dosage_guidelines.adults: N/A (pediatric focus)

warnings: First-line for non-severe pneumonia in children per WHO guidelines
Seek urgent care if breathing worsens or oxygen saturation drops
Complete full course — do not stop when fever settles

name: Rotavirus Vaccine (Rotarix / RotaTeq)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Not applicable
dosage_guidelines.children: Rotarix: 2 doses at 6 and 10 weeks; RotaTeq: 3 doses at 2, 4, 6 months

warnings: Oral vaccine — do not inject
Administer only within specified age window (first dose by 15 weeks)
Delay if infant has moderate to severe diarrhea

name: Erythromycin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg 4 times daily

warnings: Most effective in early catarrhal stage — reduces duration and spread
Azithromycin preferred in neonates (erythromycin causes IHPS)
Isolate patient until 5 days of antibiotic therapy completed

name: DPT Vaccine (Diphtheria-Pertussis-Tetanus)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Booster (Td) every 10 years
dosage_guidelines.children: Primary series: 6, 10, 14 weeks + booster at 18 months and 5 years
dosage_guidelines.duration: Full primary series; boosters as per schedule

warnings: Not a treatment — preventive vaccine
For active diphtheria: diphtheria antitoxin and penicillin/erythromycin required
Maintain cold chain for vaccine storage

name: Oral Polio Vaccine (OPV)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Not routinely used (IPV for adults)
dosage_guidelines.children: At birth, 6, 10, 14 weeks + booster doses per national schedule

warnings: Rare but real risk of VAPP — most countries now switching to IPV
Do not give to immunocompromised children — use IPV instead
Maintain cold chain at 2–8°C

name: Fenofibrate

dosage_guidelines.adults: 145 mg once daily with food

warnings: Monitor liver function during first year
Myopathy risk increases when combined with statin
Adjust dose in renal impairment

name: Silymarin (Milk Thistle)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 140 mg 3 times daily (standardized 70–80% silymarin)

warnings: Supplement — not a replacement for lifestyle modification
Avoid in patients with hormone-sensitive conditions (estrogen-like activity)
May interact with blood-thinning medications

name: Allopurinol

dosage_guidelines.adults: 100 mg once daily initially; increase to 300–600 mg/day

side_effects: skin rash (including severe SJS in Asian patients)
warnings: Screen for HLA-B*5801 allele in Southeast Asian patients — risk of severe skin reaction
Start colchicine or NSAID prophylaxis when initiating allopurinol to prevent flares
Reduce dose significantly in renal impairment

name: Colchicine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 mg then 0.5 mg one hour later (acute); 0.5–1 mg/day for prophylaxis
dosage_guidelines.duration: 1–2 days for acute attack; prophylactic up to 6 months

warnings: Reduce dose in renal or hepatic impairment
Serious toxicity with clarithromycin or cyclosporine — avoid combination
Dose-limiting side effect is diarrhea — stop if severe GI symptoms occur

name: Rosuvastatin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 5–40 mg once daily

warnings: Most potent statin — use lowest effective dose
Avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding
Asian patients may require lower starting dose due to higher plasma levels

name: Clotrimazole (Topical)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply thin layer twice daily to affected area

warnings: For external use only — avoid eyes
Complete full course even if rash improves early
Oral antifungal needed for extensive or nail involvement

name: Permethrin 5% Cream

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply to entire body from neck down; leave 8–14 hours; rinse
dosage_guidelines.children: > 2 months: apply to face, scalp, neck too
dosage_guidelines.duration: Single application; repeat in 1 week if needed

warnings: Treat all household contacts simultaneously
Wash all clothes and bedding in hot water on treatment day
Itching may persist 2–4 weeks after successful treatment (hypersensitivity reaction)

name: Methotrexate (Low dose)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 7.5–25 mg once weekly (oral/SC)

warnings: Take folic acid 5 mg weekly on a different day
Liver biopsy recommended after cumulative dose of 1.5 g
Strictly avoid pregnancy during treatment and for 3 months after stopping

name: Hydrocortisone Cream 1%

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply thin layer 1–2 times daily to affected area
dosage_guidelines.children: Apply sparingly once daily (avoid face in children)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Up to 7 days for acute flare; intermittent use for chronic

warnings: Avoid prolonged use on face, groin, or underarms — skin thinning
Do not use on infected skin — treat infection first
Use emollients liberally alongside steroid cream

name: Tretinoin (Topical)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 0.025–0.1% cream or gel; apply pea-sized amount at night
dosage_guidelines.duration: 8–12 weeks for initial response; long-term maintenance

warnings: Avoid sun exposure — use SPF 30+ daily
Initial worsening of acne (purging) is normal in first 4 weeks
Strictly contraindicated in pregnancy — use barrier contraception

name: Doxycycline

dosage_guidelines.adults: 100 mg twice daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: 7 days (or until 3 days after fever subsides)

warnings: Drug of choice for scrub typhus — start empirically if suspected (do not wait for test results)
Avoid in children under 8 and pregnant women — use azithromycin instead
Sit upright 30 min after taking and drink plenty of water

name: Paracetamol (Supportive)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500–1000 mg every 4–6 hours (max 4 g/day)

warnings: Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin — Zika can cause bleeding complications
No specific antiviral available for Zika — supportive care only
Pregnant women with Zika require specialist monitoring — fetal microcephaly risk

name: Pantoprazole

dosage_guidelines.adults: 40 mg once daily before breakfast
dosage_guidelines.duration: 4–8 weeks; long-term if needed for chronic gastritis

warnings: Long-term use increases risk of C. diff, magnesium deficiency, and bone fractures
Do not crush enteric-coated tablets
Rule out H. pylori in chronic gastritis before starting long-term PPI

name: Omeprazole + Clarithromycin + Amoxicillin
symptoms_treated: pain relieved by eating or antacids

dosage_guidelines.adults: Omeprazole 20 mg + Clarithromycin 500 mg + Amoxicillin 1 g all twice daily

warnings: Confirm H. pylori eradication 4 weeks after treatment with stool antigen test
Increasing clarithromycin resistance — check local guidelines
Avoid alcohol and NSAIDs during treatment

name: Mebeverine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 135 mg 3 times daily 20 min before meals

warnings: Not effective for all IBS subtypes — assess response in 4 weeks
Combine with dietary fiber and stress management for best results
Avoid in intestinal obstruction

name: Metronidazole

dosage_guidelines.adults: 400–500 mg 3 times daily

warnings: Strictly avoid alcohol during and 48 hours after treatment — severe reaction
Can cause peripheral neuropathy with prolonged use
Not effective for viral gastroenteritis

name: N-Acetylcysteine (NAC)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 600 mg twice daily (oral)
dosage_guidelines.children: Not typically used in occupational lung disease

warnings: No cure exists for silicosis — prevention (dust control) is primary
Remove from further silica exposure immediately
Refer for lung transplant assessment in end-stage disease

name: Metformin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg twice daily with meals; may increase to 1500–2000 mg/day
dosage_guidelines.duration: Long-term or until conception goal achieved

warnings: Start low and increase slowly to minimize GI side effects
Not first-line for infertility — ovulation induction with clomiphene preferred
Discontinue if kidney function deteriorates (eGFR < 30)

name: Combined Oral Contraceptive Pill (COCP)

dosage_guidelines.adults: One tablet daily (continuous or cyclic 21/7 regimen)
dosage_guidelines.children: Adolescents with endometriosis under specialist care

warnings: Contraindicated in smokers over 35 — increased clot risk
Does not cure endometriosis — suppresses symptoms
Breakthrough bleeding common in first 3 months

name: Ceftriaxone

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg IM single dose (or 1 g if >150 kg)

warnings: Always treat for chlamydia concurrently (co-infection common)
Test all sexual partners and refer for treatment
Increasing ceftriaxone resistance — test sensitivity where available

name: Sumatriptan

dosage_guidelines.adults: 50–100 mg oral at onset; may repeat after 2 hours (max 200 mg/day)

warnings: Do not use if history of coronary artery disease or stroke
Do not use more than 10 days/month — risk of medication overuse headache
Avoid combining with MAOIs or other triptans

name: Amitriptyline (Low Dose)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10–25 mg at bedtime (preventive)

warnings: Take at bedtime — significant sedation
Avoid in cardiac arrhythmia or recent heart attack
Do not combine with MAOIs — risk of serotonin syndrome

name: Amoxicillin-Clavulanate (Augmentin)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 875/125 mg twice daily

warnings: Covers beta-lactamase producing organisms (H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis)
Diarrhea more common than plain amoxicillin due to clavulanate
Avoid in penicillin allergy

name: Cetirizine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10 mg once daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: Seasonal or perennial; daily during allergy season

warnings: Less sedating than first-generation antihistamines but mild drowsiness possible
Avoid alcohol — increases sedation
Dose reduction in severe renal impairment

name: Benzylpenicillin (Penicillin G)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 600 mg IM/IV every 6 hours (severe)
dosage_guidelines.duration: 10 days (oral penicillin V for mild cases)

warnings: Drug of choice for Group A Strep tonsillitis
Full 10-day course prevents rheumatic fever
Use oral phenoxymethylpenicillin for mild/moderate cases

name: Ciprofloxacin Ear Drops

dosage_guidelines.adults: 4 drops in affected ear twice daily

warnings: Ensure eardrum integrity before use if not familiar with chronic ear disease
Warm drops in hand before using to reduce dizziness
Treat underlying cause — ENT referral for perforations

name: Epley Maneuver (Non-pharmacological) + Betahistine

dosage_guidelines.adults: Betahistine 16 mg 3 times daily (adjunct to Epley maneuver)

warnings: Epley maneuver is first-line — pharmacotherapy is adjunct only
Betahistine not proven superior to placebo in BPPV alone
Persistent vertigo beyond 4 weeks warrants MRI to rule out central cause

name: Ciprofloxacin Eye Drops

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1–2 drops in affected eye every 2 hours while awake (day 1–2); then 4 times/day

warnings: Do not use for viral conjunctivitis — antibiotics are not effective
Wash hands before and after instilling drops
Contact lenses should not be worn during treatment

name: Artificial Tears (Carboxymethylcellulose)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1–2 drops in each eye 3–6 times daily as needed

warnings: Preservative-free formulation preferred for frequent use (>4 times/day)
Screen time breaks (20-20-20 rule) alongside drops
Rule out underlying Sjogren's syndrome or blepharitis

name: Latanoprost Eye Drops

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 drop in affected eye once daily in the evening

warnings: Only for evening use — morning dosing reduces efficacy
Inform patients of cosmetic changes (iris darkening, lash growth)
Monitor IOP every 3–6 months

name: Nitrofurantoin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 100 mg modified-release twice daily or 50 mg 4 times daily

warnings: Avoid in late pregnancy (from 38 weeks) and neonates — hemolytic anemia risk
Contraindicated if eGFR < 30 — poor efficacy and accumulation
Take with food to minimize GI side effects

name: Tamsulosin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 0.4 mg once daily after same meal each day

warnings: Take after the same meal daily for consistent drug levels
Risk of intraoperative floppy iris syndrome — inform ophthalmologist before eye surgery
Avoid if hypersensitive to sulfonamides

name: Mefenamic Acid

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg 3 times daily with food; start 1–2 days before period

warnings: Take with food to minimize GI effects
Avoid if history of peptic ulcer, kidney disease, or aspirin allergy
Limit use — start day before expected period for maximum benefit

name: Diclofenac

dosage_guidelines.adults: 50 mg 2–3 times daily with food; topical gel 2–4 g per area 4 times/day
dosage_guidelines.duration: Acute: 3–5 days; chronic: as needed with GI protection

warnings: Use PPI (omeprazole) concurrently in patients with GI risk
Avoid in heart failure or post-MI
Topical formulation preferred for localized pain to reduce systemic effects

name: Pregabalin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 75 mg twice daily; increase to 150 mg twice daily after 1 week (max 600 mg/day)
dosage_guidelines.children: Not approved under 18 for neuropathic pain
dosage_guidelines.duration: As long as nerve pain persists; taper to stop

warnings: Do not stop abruptly — seizure risk (in epilepsy patients) and withdrawal symptoms
Avoid driving until you know how drowsy it makes you
Risk of abuse — monitor in patients with substance use history

name: Fluconazole

dosage_guidelines.adults: 150 mg single dose (oropharyngeal): 100 mg once daily for 7–14 days
dosage_guidelines.duration: 7–14 days depending on severity and immunity

warnings: Strong drug interaction with warfarin — INR can spike dangerously
Hepatotoxic in high doses — monitor liver in prolonged courses
Address underlying immunosuppression for recurrent thrush

name: Lactulose

dosage_guidelines.adults: 15–30 mL (10–20 g) once or twice daily; adjust to produce 2 soft stools/day
dosage_guidelines.children: 1–5 years: 5 mL twice daily; 6–12 years: 10 mL twice daily

warnings: Start with lower dose and titrate up to avoid excessive diarrhea
Contains lactose — use with caution in galactosemia
May take 24–48 hours for first effect — inform patient

name: Omeprazole

dosage_guidelines.adults: 20–40 mg once daily before breakfast
dosage_guidelines.duration: 4–8 weeks acute; ongoing for erosive GERD

warnings: Long-term use increases fracture risk and C. diff infection
Do not use with clopidogrel — reduces antiplatelet effect (use pantoprazole)
Rule out H. pylori in all GERD before long-term PPI therapy

name: Loratadine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10 mg once daily
dosage_guidelines.children: 2–5 years: 5 mg once daily; ≥ 6 years: 10 mg once daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: Daily during allergy season or ongoing

warnings: Non-sedating — safe for daytime use and driving
Dose reduction in severe liver or kidney disease
Not effective for food allergy anaphylaxis — use epinephrine for that

name: Zolpidem

dosage_guidelines.adults: 5–10 mg immediately before bedtime; max 10 mg/day

warnings: High risk of dependence and rebound insomnia — limit to short-term use
Never drive the night of use or the next morning
Reduce to 5 mg in elderly women — slower drug clearance

name: Dimenhydrinate
symptoms_treated: sweating and pallor from motion sickness

dosage_guidelines.adults: 50–100 mg every 4–6 hours (max 400 mg/day); take 30 min before travel

warnings: Causes significant drowsiness — avoid driving
Avoid alcohol during use
Use with caution in glaucoma or prostate problems

name: Warfarin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2–10 mg once daily; dose guided by INR (target 2–3)
dosage_guidelines.duration: 3–6 months minimum; lifelong if recurrent DVT

warnings: Frequent INR monitoring essential — narrow therapeutic window
Many food and drug interactions — check every new prescription
Vitamin K-rich foods (spinach, broccoli) affect INR — keep intake consistent

name: Valproic Acid

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500–2500 mg/day in 2–3 divided doses
dosage_guidelines.duration: Long-term; 2–5 years seizure-free before considering discontinuation

warnings: Highly teratogenic — causes neural tube defects; avoid in women of childbearing age if possible
Monitor liver function in children under 2 — fatal hepatotoxicity risk
Pancreatitis can occur — report severe abdominal pain

name: Methylphenidate

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10–60 mg/day in divided doses or extended-release
dosage_guidelines.children: 5–60 mg/day (start 5 mg twice daily; titrate weekly)

warnings: Controlled substance — monitor for abuse and diversion
Monitor growth in children — can suppress height velocity
Do not use in patients with structural heart disease or arrhythmia

name: Lithium

dosage_guidelines.adults: 300–600 mg 2–3 times daily; titrate to serum level 0.6–1.2 mEq/L

warnings: Narrow therapeutic window — lithium toxicity can be life-threatening
Monitor levels, renal function, and thyroid every 6 months
Dehydration, NSAIDs, and diuretics can raise lithium to toxic levels

name: Haloperidol

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2–20 mg/day in divided doses; 5 mg IM for acute agitation
dosage_guidelines.duration: Long-term; 1–2 years after first episode minimum

warnings: Risk of tardive dyskinesia with long-term use — monitor with AIMS scale
Avoid in Parkinson's disease or dementia with Lewy bodies
Correct electrolytes before use — QT risk with low K+ or Mg2+

name: Minoxidil (Topical)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Men: 5% solution or foam once daily; Women: 2% twice daily or 5% once daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: Lifelong — hair loss returns within months of stopping

warnings: Hair shedding in first 4 weeks is normal — reassure patient and continue
Must be used indefinitely — not a cure
Avoid contact with face, eyes, or mucous membranes

name: Ketoconazole Shampoo 2%

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply to wet scalp; lather and leave 3–5 min; rinse — twice weekly for 4 weeks

warnings: Avoid contact with eyes
Do not use on broken or inflamed skin
For recurrent cases, alternate with zinc pyrithione shampoo

name: Albendazole

dosage_guidelines.adults: 400 mg single dose (soil-transmitted helminths)
dosage_guidelines.children: > 2 years: 400 mg single dose; 1–2 years: 200 mg single dose
dosage_guidelines.duration: Single dose; repeat in 2 weeks for pinworm; 3-day course for tissue infections

warnings: Single dose is highly effective for intestinal worms — no need for prolonged treatment
Treat all household members simultaneously for pinworm
Avoid in first trimester of pregnancy

name: Metronidazole

dosage_guidelines.adults: 800 mg 3 times daily (intestinal); 400–800 mg 3 times daily (liver abscess)
dosage_guidelines.duration: 5–10 days; followed by diloxanide furoate for luminal clearance

warnings: Always follow with a luminal agent (diloxanide furoate) to clear intestinal cysts
Strictly avoid alcohol — severe reaction
Prolonged therapy causes peripheral neuropathy — limit duration

name: Tinidazole

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2 g single dose
dosage_guidelines.duration: Single dose (tinidazole preferred over metronidazole for giardiasis)

warnings: Single dose makes compliance easy — preferred over 5-day metronidazole course
Avoid alcohol during and 3 days after treatment
Treat symptomatic family contacts to prevent reinfection

name: Silver Sulfadiazine Cream

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply 3–5 mm thickness to burn once or twice daily after cleaning
dosage_guidelines.children: Same as adults (avoid in neonates and sulfa-allergic)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Until wound heals or skin grafting is performed

warnings: Avoid in sulfa allergy
Monitor WBC — can cause transient leukopenia
May delay wound healing in some studies — consider alternatives for small burns

name: Ibuprofen

dosage_guidelines.adults: 400 mg every 6–8 hours with food (max 2400 mg/day)

warnings: Use RICE (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation) alongside medication
Avoid on empty stomach
Short-term use only — chronic use increases GI and cardiovascular risk

name: Bisacodyl

dosage_guidelines.adults: 5–10 mg orally at bedtime; 10 mg suppository as needed
dosage_guidelines.duration: Short-term; not for routine long-term use

warnings: Do not use if bowel obstruction suspected
Not for long-term daily use — laxative dependence risk
Take tablet whole — do not crush; avoid with milk or antacids (remove enteric coating)

name: Pilocarpine Eye Drops

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1–2 drops of 1–4% solution every 6–12 hours
dosage_guidelines.duration: Long-term; usually combined with other IOP-lowering agents

warnings: Not preferred for long-term open-angle glaucoma — laser or prostaglandins preferred
Causes small pupils — poor night vision
Systemic absorption can cause sweating, salivation, bradycardia

name: Anastrozole

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 mg once daily

warnings: Monitor bone density — causes osteoporosis; supplement calcium and vitamin D
Not for premenopausal women (except in combination with ovarian suppression)
Regular breast and bone density monitoring required

name: Furosemide

dosage_guidelines.adults: 20–80 mg once or twice daily (oral); 40 mg IV for acute decompensation

warnings: Monitor electrolytes regularly — hypokalemia causes arrhythmia
Excessive diuresis causes kidney injury — avoid too-rapid fluid removal
Hold if patient is dehydrated or in acute kidney injury

name: Amiodarone

dosage_guidelines.adults: 200 mg 3 times daily loading (1 week); then 200 mg twice daily (1 week); then 200 mg once daily maintenance

warnings: Monitor thyroid, liver, and pulmonary function every 6 months
Very long half-life (40–55 days) — drug interactions persist long after stopping
High-level sun protection required — blue-grey skin discoloration from sun

name: Pantoprazole

dosage_guidelines.adults: 40 mg once daily before breakfast

warnings: Flatulence is often dietary — review for high-FODMAP foods alongside medication
PPIs not routinely indicated for simple gas — rule out GERD first
Avoid long-term use without clear indication

name: Hydroxyzine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 25 mg 3–4 times daily (max 100 mg/dose)

warnings: More sedating than second-generation antihistamines — avoid driving
For anaphylaxis, epinephrine is first-line — hydroxyzine is adjunct only
Use with caution in elderly — high anticholinergic burden

name: Oxybutynin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 5 mg 2–3 times daily; extended-release 5–30 mg once daily

warnings: Avoid in elderly — significant anticholinergic burden increases fall and dementia risk
Contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention
Mirabegron is a safer alternative for elderly patients

name: Phlebotonics (Diosmin + Hesperidin)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500/50 mg twice daily or 1000 mg once daily

warnings: Not a curative treatment — compression stockings remain first-line
Surgical or endovascular treatment required for large varicosities
Discontinue if GI side effects are severe

name: Sildenafil

dosage_guidelines.adults: 50 mg orally 1 hour before sexual activity; range 25–100 mg

warnings: Absolutely contraindicated with nitrates (nitroglycerin, isosorbide) — life-threatening hypotension
Avoid in severe cardiovascular disease or recent stroke/heart attack
Vision or hearing loss — stop immediately and seek urgent care

name: Snake Antivenom (Polyvalent)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10 vials IV (initial dose); repeat based on clinical response
dosage_guidelines.children: Same dose as adults — based on venom amount, not body weight
dosage_guidelines.duration: Until neutralization of venom is achieved

warnings: Adrenaline (epinephrine) must be ready before starting antivenom
Skin testing is unreliable and can delay treatment — do not perform routinely
Start antivenom only for systemic envenomation — not all snakebites require antivenom

name: Scopolamine (Transdermal)
symptoms_treated: vomiting from sea or air travel

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1.5 mg patch applied behind ear 4 hours before travel; effective 72 hours
dosage_guidelines.duration: One patch per 72 hours of travel

warnings: Wash hands after handling patch — accidental eye contact causes pupil dilation
Avoid in narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention
Remove patch before MRI — metallic backing may cause burns

name: Vitamin B12 (Cyanocobalamin)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1000 mcg IM weekly for 4 weeks, then monthly; or 1000 mcg oral daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: Lifelong if malabsorption; 3–6 months for dietary deficiency

warnings: IM injection preferred if intrinsic factor deficiency (pernicious anemia)
Folic acid can mask B12 deficiency — always check B12 before giving folate
Monitor potassium when starting in severe deficiency — hypokalemia risk

name: Gabapentin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 300 mg at bedtime initially; increase to 300 mg 3 times daily (max 2400 mg/day for fibromyalgia)

warnings: Do not stop abruptly — seizure risk (in epilepsy patients)
Suicidal ideation risk — monitor mood
Abuse potential — monitor in those with substance use history

name: Fludrocortisone

dosage_guidelines.adults: 0.05–0.2 mg once daily

warnings: Monitor blood pressure and potassium regularly
Combine with non-pharmacological measures (compression stockings, high salt diet, hydration)
Contraindicated in uncontrolled hypertension or congestive heart failure

name: Melatonin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 0.5–5 mg 30–60 minutes before bedtime

warnings: Light therapy is first-line for SAD — melatonin is adjunct
Avoid combining with sedatives or alcohol — increased sedation
Long-term safety data is limited — use minimum effective dose

name: Amoxicillin-Clavulanate

dosage_guidelines.adults: 875/125 mg twice daily

warnings: Most acute bronchitis is viral — antibiotics only if bacterial signs present
Diarrhea more common than plain amoxicillin
Avoid in penicillin allergy

name: Salbutamol Nebulization

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2.5 mg nebulized every 4–6 hours

warnings: Overuse can mask worsening disease — reassess frequently
Monitor heart rate in elderly and cardiac patients
Not a substitute for steroid inhaler in recurrent cases

name: Methylprednisolone (IV)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1–2 mg/kg/day IV in divided doses (early moderate ARDS)
dosage_guidelines.duration: 7–28 days with taper; timing and duration per protocol

warnings: Used selectively — benefit only in certain ARDS phases (not within first 24 hours)
Monitor blood glucose closely — steroids worsen hyperglycemia
Prone positioning and lung-protective ventilation are primary interventions

name: Cisatracurium (Neuromuscular Blocker)
symptoms_treated: severe hypoxia in mechanically ventilated patients

dosage_guidelines.adults: 0.1–0.2 mg/kg IV bolus; 1–3 mcg/kg/min infusion
dosage_guidelines.duration: Up to 48 hours in severe ARDS (ACURASYS protocol)

warnings: Requires mechanical ventilation and continuous monitoring — ICU only
Reverse with neostigmine when appropriate
Prolonged use causes ICU-acquired weakness — limit duration

name: Pirfenidone

dosage_guidelines.adults: 267 mg 3 times daily (week 1–2); 534 mg 3 times daily (week 3–4); 801 mg 3 times daily (maintenance)

warnings: Use high SPF sunscreen daily — severe photosensitivity reactions possible
Monitor liver function at weeks 1, 3, 6, then every 3 months
Smoking reduces drug efficacy — strongly encourage smoking cessation

name: Nintedanib

dosage_guidelines.adults: 150 mg twice daily with food

warnings: Diarrhea in up to 62% — manage with loperamide and hydration; dose reduce if severe
Teratogenic — women of childbearing age must use effective contraception
Monitor liver function monthly for first 3 months, then every 3 months

name: Sacubitril-Valsartan (Entresto)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Start 24/26 mg twice daily; target 97/103 mg twice daily

warnings: Do not use within 36 hours of ACE inhibitor — risk of angioedema
Monitor blood pressure, potassium, and renal function
Contraindicated in pregnancy — causes fetal harm

name: Bisoprolol
symptoms_treated: rapid heart rate in heart failure

dosage_guidelines.adults: Start 1.25 mg once daily; target 10 mg once daily (titrate slowly)

warnings: Do not start during acute decompensated heart failure
Never stop abruptly — gradual taper required
Avoid in severe asthma or second/third degree heart block

name: Digoxin
symptoms_treated: rapid ventricular rate in atrial fibrillation

dosage_guidelines.adults: 0.125–0.25 mg once daily (rate control in AF)

warnings: Narrow therapeutic window — check digoxin levels regularly (target 0.5–0.9 ng/mL)
Toxicity worsened by low potassium — maintain K+ > 4 mEq/L
Many drug interactions — check before adding any new medication

name: Rivaroxaban

dosage_guidelines.adults: 20 mg once daily with evening meal (AF stroke prevention)
dosage_guidelines.children: Not approved for AF in children

warnings: No routine monitoring required but bleeding risk is real
Contraindicated in significant renal impairment (CrCl < 15 mL/min)
Reversal agent: Andexanet alfa — available in major hospitals

name: Cilostazol

dosage_guidelines.adults: 100 mg twice daily 30 min before or 2 hours after meals

warnings: Contraindicated in any degree of heart failure
Avoid grapefruit juice — increases drug levels
Check for drug interactions with CYP3A4 inhibitors (diltiazem, erythromycin)

name: Aspirin + Clopidogrel

dosage_guidelines.adults: Aspirin 75–100 mg + Clopidogrel 75 mg once daily

warnings: Dual antiplatelet increases bleeding risk — add PPI for GI protection
Avoid if recent GI bleed or surgery
Regular vascular review required alongside medical therapy

name: Rivaroxaban

dosage_guidelines.adults: 15 mg twice daily for 21 days; then 20 mg once daily with food
dosage_guidelines.duration: 3–6 months minimum; longer if unprovoked DVT

warnings: Take 20 mg dose with food for full absorption
Avoid in severe renal impairment
Spinal/epidural procedures require dose holding — consult hematology

name: Heparin (Low Molecular Weight - Enoxaparin)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 mg/kg SC twice daily or 1.5 mg/kg once daily (treatment)
dosage_guidelines.children: 1 mg/kg every 12 hours (adjust per anti-Xa level)
dosage_guidelines.duration: 5–10 days then transition to oral anticoagulant

warnings: Monitor platelets — heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) is life-threatening
Dose adjustment in renal impairment — use unfractionated heparin if eGFR < 30
Preferred in pregnancy over oral anticoagulants

name: Acarbose

dosage_guidelines.adults: 25 mg 3 times daily with first bite of meal; max 100 mg 3 times daily

warnings: GI side effects are common and dose-dependent — start low and titrate slowly
Not for use in inflammatory bowel disease or bowel obstruction
Lifestyle changes (diet + exercise) remain the cornerstone of pre-diabetes management

name: Metformin
symptoms_treated: weight gain related to glucose dysregulation

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg twice daily with meals; max 2000 mg/day

warnings: Second-line after lifestyle modification fails in pre-diabetes
Hold in procedures with contrast dye
Monitor renal function every 6–12 months

name: Dextrose 50% IV
symptoms_treated: loss of consciousness from low blood sugar

dosage_guidelines.adults: 50 mL of 50% dextrose IV bolus (25 g glucose)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Single dose; repeat if glucose remains < 4 mmol/L after 15 min

warnings: Use large vein — 50% dextrose is highly concentrated and causes tissue necrosis if it leaks
Check blood glucose 15 minutes after dose and repeat if needed
Identify and treat the cause of hypoglycemia — do not just treat the episode

name: Glucagon
symptoms_treated: loss of consciousness from low sugar

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 mg IM/SC/IV; intranasal: 3 mg one spray in one nostril
dosage_guidelines.duration: Single dose; oral carbohydrates after patient recovers

warnings: Ineffective in starvation or adrenal insufficiency — use dextrose IV instead
Patient should eat after recovery to prevent relapse
Intranasal formulation (Baqsimi) is needle-free and for home use

name: Pancreatic Enzyme Replacement (Creon)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 25,000–80,000 units lipase per meal; half dose with snacks
dosage_guidelines.duration: Lifelong in chronic pancreatitis with exocrine insufficiency

warnings: Take with every meal and snack — not effective if taken after eating
Do not crush or chew enteric-coated capsules
High doses can cause fibrosing colonopathy in children with CF — do not exceed 10,000 units/kg/day

name: Morphine (IV)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2–4 mg IV every 4 hours or PCA pump; titrate to pain
dosage_guidelines.duration: Until pain adequately controlled; switch to oral as soon as tolerated

warnings: Respiratory depression risk — have naloxone available
Use with caution in liver failure and renal impairment
Opioid-induced constipation should be treated prophylactically

name: Ursodeoxycholic Acid (UDCA)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 8–10 mg/kg/day in 2–3 divided doses
dosage_guidelines.duration: 6–24 months for dissolution; ongoing for prevention

warnings: Only effective for small, non-calcified cholesterol gallstones
Gallstones recur in 50% within 5 years after stopping — maintenance may be needed
Surgery (cholecystectomy) is more definitive — UDCA is for non-surgical candidates

name: Ciprofloxacin + Metronidazole

dosage_guidelines.adults: Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV every 12 hours + Metronidazole 500 mg IV every 8 hours

warnings: Antibiotics are bridging therapy — cholecystectomy is the definitive treatment
Avoid alcohol with metronidazole
Perform blood cultures and sensitivity testing to guide antibiotic choice

name: Lactase Enzyme Supplement

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1–2 tablets (3000–9000 FCC units) with first bite of dairy food
dosage_guidelines.children: Half adult dose (age and weight appropriate)
dosage_guidelines.duration: As needed with each lactose-containing meal

warnings: Not a cure — reduces symptoms only when taken with dairy
Does not help symptoms from non-dairy lactose sources (medications, breads)
Consider calcium supplementation if dairy is significantly restricted

name: Ribavirin + Supportive Care

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2 g loading then 1 g every 6 hours for 4 days, then 500 mg every 8 hours for 6 days (Lassa/CCHF)

warnings: Effective for Lassa fever and Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever — not all VHFs
Strict barrier nursing required — these diseases are highly contagious
Contraindicated in pregnancy despite clinical need — balance risks

name: Potassium Iodide

dosage_guidelines.adults: 150–300 mcg iodine daily via iodized salt or supplementation
dosage_guidelines.duration: Ongoing via iodized salt; therapeutic course as per physician

side_effects: iodism (metallic taste, runny nose at excess doses)
warnings: Treat underlying cause — iodine deficiency vs. autonomous nodule vs. autoimmune
Excess iodine can trigger Jod-Basedow hyperthyroidism in at-risk patients
Ultrasound and thyroid function tests guide treatment decisions

name: Levothyroxine
symptoms_treated: TSH suppression to reduce nodule growth

dosage_guidelines.adults: Suppression: 1.5–2 mcg/kg/day to keep TSH 0.1–0.5 mIU/L

warnings: TSH suppression increases risk of osteoporosis and atrial fibrillation
Not recommended for routine nodule treatment without hypothyroidism
Biopsy (FNA) must rule out malignancy before medical management

name: Ciprofloxacin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg twice daily (oral) or 400 mg IV twice daily (severe)

warnings: Complete the full course — prostatic penetration requires extended therapy
Avoid excessive sun exposure
Tendon rupture risk especially in patients over 60 or on corticosteroids

name: Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline

dosage_guidelines.adults: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose + Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily + Metronidazole 400 mg twice daily

warnings: Treat all sexual partners — STI source must be addressed
Hospitalize if no improvement in 72 hours or signs of peritonitis
Untreated PID leads to infertility and ectopic pregnancy risk

name: Metronidazole Vaginal Gel

dosage_guidelines.adults: One applicatorful (5 g) intravaginally once or twice daily
dosage_guidelines.children: Not indicated (use oral metronidazole in adolescents with guidance)

warnings: Avoid alcohol during treatment (systemic absorption does occur)
Concomitant yeast infection common — may need antifungal after course
Partner treatment not routinely recommended for BV but advised if recurrent

name: Clotrimazole Vaginal Pessary

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg pessary single dose intravaginally at bedtime (or 200 mg for 3 nights)
dosage_guidelines.children: Adolescents as per adult dosing with guidance

warnings: Avoid during menstruation — absorption reduced
Condoms may be damaged by clotrimazole — use alternative contraception
Recurrent thrush (>4 episodes/year) requires oral fluconazole and investigation

name: Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6) + Wrist Splint
symptoms_treated: tingling in thumb and first 3 fingers

dosage_guidelines.adults: 100 mg once daily (B6 as adjunct; evidence limited)
dosage_guidelines.duration: 3 months trial alongside wrist splinting; refer for injection or surgery if needed

warnings: Do not exceed 100 mg/day — paradoxically causes neuropathy at high doses
Wrist splinting at night is the primary conservative treatment
Corticosteroid injection or surgical release if conservative measures fail

name: Corticosteroid Injection (Methylprednisolone)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 20–40 mg methylprednisolone + local anesthetic; 1–3 injections
dosage_guidelines.duration: 1 injection per session; repeat at 4–6 week intervals if needed (max 3)

warnings: Limit to 3 injections — repeated injections weaken tendon and increase rupture risk
Rest elbow for 48 hours after injection
Physiotherapy and eccentric exercises are primary long-term management

name: Corticosteroid Injection (Triamcinolone)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 20–40 mg triamcinolone + lidocaine injection into tender point
dosage_guidelines.duration: Single injection; may repeat at 6 weeks (max 3 total)

warnings: Avoid injection directly into tendon — risk of rupture
Monitor blood glucose in diabetics post-injection
Eccentric strengthening exercises are the cornerstone of long-term recovery

name: Ibuprofen

dosage_guidelines.adults: 400–600 mg 3 times daily with food

warnings: Rule out septic bursitis (infected bursa) before starting NSAIDs — needs aspiration and antibiotics
Take with food to protect stomach
Combine with ice and rest for best results in acute bursitis

name: Naproxen

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg twice daily with food

warnings: NSAIDs may delay tendon healing with prolonged use — limit duration
Eccentric exercises are the most effective long-term tendinitis treatment
Avoid corticosteroid injection in Achilles tendinitis — high rupture risk

name: Baclofen

dosage_guidelines.adults: 5 mg 3 times daily; titrate to 25 mg 3 times daily (max 80 mg/day)
dosage_guidelines.children: 5 years+: 2.5 mg 3 times daily; titrate based on response
dosage_guidelines.duration: Short to medium term for spasm; physiotherapy is primary treatment

warnings: Do not stop abruptly — can cause hallucinations and seizures
Avoid driving — significant sedation
Physical therapy, bracing, and surgery are primary for structural kyphosis — drugs treat symptoms only

name: Diazepam
symptoms_treated: anxiety related to chronic scoliosis pain

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2–5 mg 2–3 times daily for spasm
dosage_guidelines.children: 0.04–0.3 mg/kg per dose (specialist guided for spasm)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Short-term only; bracing and surgery are primary treatments

warnings: High dependence potential — use minimum effective dose for shortest duration
Avoid in respiratory compromise (scoliosis-related restrictive lung disease)
Bracing in adolescents and surgical correction are definitive treatments for scoliosis

name: Lamotrigine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 25 mg once daily (weeks 1–2); 25 mg twice daily (weeks 3–4); target 100–200 mg twice daily
dosage_guidelines.children: 0.15 mg/kg/day titrating to 1–5 mg/kg/day (with valproate) or 5–15 mg/kg/day (without)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Long-term; 2–5 years seizure-free before considering taper

warnings: Titrate slowly — rapid escalation increases risk of severe rash (SJS/TEN)
Stop immediately at first sign of rash — may be life-threatening
Enzyme inducers (carbamazepine) halve its levels; valproate doubles them — dose adjustment required

name: Carbamazepine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 100–200 mg twice daily initially; titrate to 400–1200 mg/day
dosage_guidelines.children: 5–10 mg/kg/day; titrate to 10–30 mg/kg/day in divided doses

side_effects: severe skin reactions in HLA-B*1502 carriers
warnings: Test for HLA-B*1502 in Southeast Asian patients before prescribing — risk of fatal SJS
Potent enzyme inducer — reduces levels of many drugs including OCP and warfarin
Monitor sodium — causes SIADH and hyponatremia

name: Prednisolone
symptoms_treated: inability to close eye on affected side

dosage_guidelines.adults: 50–60 mg once daily for 5 days; taper over 5 more days (total 10 days)
dosage_guidelines.duration: 10 days; start within 72 hours of onset for best results

warnings: Start within 72 hours of symptom onset — delays reduce efficacy
Use eye lubricants and tape to protect the exposed cornea
Antiviral (acyclovir) may be added but benefit over prednisolone alone is debated

name: Atomoxetine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 40 mg once daily initially; target 80–100 mg/day after 3 days
dosage_guidelines.children: < 70 kg: 0.5 mg/kg/day; increase to 1.2 mg/kg/day after 3 days

warnings: Non-stimulant — preferred in patients with substance abuse history
Monitor for suicidal ideation in children and adolescents
Hepatotoxicity reported — stop if jaundice or dark urine develops

name: Risperidone

dosage_guidelines.adults: 0.5–3 mg/day (behavioral symptoms)
dosage_guidelines.children: < 20 kg: 0.25 mg/day; ≥ 20 kg: 0.5 mg/day; titrate slowly

warnings: Only FDA-approved for irritability in autism — does not treat core autism symptoms
Monitor weight and metabolic parameters regularly
Behavioral therapy is primary intervention — risperidone is adjunct

name: Quetiapine

dosage_guidelines.adults: Mania: 400–800 mg/day; Depression: 300 mg/day; start 50 mg twice daily

warnings: Metabolic monitoring required — weight, glucose, lipids at baseline and every 3 months
Causes significant sedation — take at night
Risk of tardive dyskinesia with long-term use

name: Olanzapine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 5–10 mg once daily initially; target 10–20 mg/day
dosage_guidelines.duration: Long-term; minimum 2 years after first episode

warnings: Highest metabolic risk of all antipsychotics — monitor weight, glucose, and lipids
Do not use in elderly with dementia — increased stroke and death risk
IM formulation used for acute agitation — do not combine with benzodiazepine IM

name: Naltrexone

dosage_guidelines.adults: 50 mg once daily orally or 380 mg IM once monthly (extended-release)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Long-term; minimum 12 months for alcohol use disorder

warnings: Contraindicated in patients currently using opioids — precipitates acute withdrawal
Test for opioid use before starting — urine drug screen required
Liver function monitoring recommended — hepatotoxic at high doses

name: Buprenorphine + Naloxone (Suboxone)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Day 1: 2–4 mg; titrate to 8–24 mg/day based on withdrawal score

warnings: Start only when patient is in mild-moderate withdrawal — precipitated withdrawal risk if started too early
Naloxone component deters IV misuse — film must be placed under tongue
Driving impairment possible early in treatment

name: Aloe Vera Gel + Cooling Lotion

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply liberally to affected area 3–4 times daily
dosage_guidelines.children: Same as adults; avoid products with alcohol or benzocaine in children

side_effects: delayed healing if thick creams trap heat
warnings: Avoid further sun exposure until healed
Do not apply to blistered skin without medical advice
Seek medical care if blisters are widespread, fever develops, or skin is very painful

name: Hydroquinone 4% Cream

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply twice daily to affected areas only (morning and night)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Maximum 3–6 months; then 2-month break to prevent ochronosis

warnings: Always use SPF 50+ sunscreen — sun exposure reverses treatment
Do not apply to healthy surrounding skin
Stop immediately if paradoxical darkening occurs — ochronosis is irreversible

name: Tacrolimus Ointment 0.1%

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply twice daily to affected patches
dosage_guidelines.children: 0.03% strength for children 2–15 years; twice daily

side_effects: rare increased cancer risk (theoretical with long-term use)
warnings: Avoid prolonged sun exposure — cover treated areas
Black box warning: theoretical lymphoma risk (causality not established)
Phototherapy (NBUVB) is often more effective and combined with tacrolimus

name: Acetic Acid Ear Drops 2%

dosage_guidelines.adults: 4–6 drops in affected ear 3–4 times daily

warnings: Do not use if eardrum is perforated
Warm drops to body temperature before using to prevent dizziness
Keep ear dry — no swimming during treatment

name: Betahistine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 16 mg 3 times daily or 24 mg twice daily

warnings: Avoid in pheochromocytoma — may cause hypertensive crisis
Use with caution in peptic ulcer disease
Not effective for all causes of hearing loss — diagnosis must confirm Meniere's disease

name: Mometasone Furoate Nasal Spray

dosage_guidelines.adults: 200 mcg (2 sprays each nostril) once daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: Long-term; 3–6 months minimum to assess polyp reduction

warnings: Use correct technique — direct spray toward outer wall of nose, not septum
Nasal polyps may require endoscopic sinus surgery if unresponsive to steroids
Regular follow-up with ENT for monitoring

name: Collagenase Ointment (Santyl)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply once daily directly to wound (after debridement)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Until wound bed is clean; then switch to moist wound dressings

warnings: Do not use with antiseptics containing heavy metals (silver, iodine) — inactivates enzyme
Protect surrounding healthy skin with zinc oxide paste
Nutritional optimization (protein, vitamin C, zinc) is essential for wound healing

name: Piperacillin-Tazobactam (Pip-Taz)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 4.5 g IV every 6–8 hours (extended infusion over 4 hours)
dosage_guidelines.duration: 7–10 days; de-escalate based on culture and sensitivity

warnings: De-escalate to narrow-spectrum antibiotic once culture results available
Extended infusion (4-hour) improves outcomes in severe sepsis
Combination with vancomycin increases nephrotoxicity risk — monitor renal function

name: Norepinephrine

dosage_guidelines.adults: 0.01–3 mcg/kg/min IV infusion; titrate to MAP ≥ 65 mmHg
dosage_guidelines.duration: Until hemodynamic stability achieved; wean gradually

warnings: First-line vasopressor in septic shock per Surviving Sepsis Campaign
Administer via central venous catheter only — peripheral extravasation causes tissue death
Monitor urine output and lactate clearance as perfusion markers

name: Oxybutynin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 5 mg 2–3 times daily; extended-release 5–30 mg once daily
dosage_guidelines.children: 5+ years: 5 mg twice daily (specialist guidance for nocturnal enuresis)

warnings: Avoid in elderly — anticholinergic burden increases fall and cognitive risk
Contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma and urinary retention
Mirabegron is a safer option for elderly with OAB

name: Loperamide

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2 mg after each loose stool (max 16 mg/day)
dosage_guidelines.children: Not routinely used for fecal incontinence in children
dosage_guidelines.duration: As needed to control stool consistency

warnings: Do not use if infectious diarrhea suspected — can worsen infection
Overdose can cause cardiac arrhythmia — do not exceed recommended dose
Biofeedback and pelvic floor therapy are primary treatments for fecal incontinence

name: Amoxicillin

dosage_guidelines.adults: 875/125 mg (Augmentin) twice daily; or amoxicillin 500 mg 3 times daily

warnings: Always assess rabies prophylaxis need — dog vaccination history critical
Clean wound thoroughly with soap and water for 5–10 minutes immediately
Consider tetanus immunization status

name: IV Fluid Replacement (Normal Saline)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500–1000 mL isotonic saline IV rapidly; then reassess
dosage_guidelines.children: 20 mL/kg IV bolus; repeat if needed
dosage_guidelines.duration: Until clinical improvement; then oral hydration

warnings: Move patient to cool environment and remove excess clothing first
Monitor closely — heat exhaustion can progress to heat stroke (emergency)
Add oral electrolyte replacement once patient can tolerate oral intake

name: Warmed IV Fluids + Active Warming

dosage_guidelines.adults: Warmed IV saline 42°C at 150–200 mL/hr; active external rewarming
dosage_guidelines.children: Same principle — warm IV fluids and external warming devices

warnings: Handle gently — hypothermic heart is highly prone to fatal arrhythmia
No patient is dead until warm and dead — CPR should continue until core temp normalized
ECMO may be needed for severe hypothermia with cardiac arrest

name: Epinephrine (Adrenaline) Auto-injector

dosage_guidelines.adults: 0.5 mg IM (0.5 mL of 1:1000) into outer thigh; repeat after 5 minutes if needed
dosage_guidelines.children: < 25 kg: 0.15 mg; ≥ 25 kg: 0.3 mg (EpiPen Jr / EpiPen)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Single dose as emergency; always seek emergency care after

warnings: Epinephrine is the only first-line treatment for anaphylaxis — antihistamines and steroids are NOT substitutes
Always go to emergency room after using EpiPen — biphasic reaction possible 8–72 hours later
Train patient and caregivers on EpiPen technique before prescribing

name: Hydrocortisone Cream + Emollient

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply 1% hydrocortisone to affected area twice daily; apply emollient liberally throughout day
dosage_guidelines.duration: 7–14 days for acute; identify and avoid trigger

warnings: Identify and remove the causative allergen or irritant — essential for resolution
Stronger steroids for severe reactions (under medical supervision only)
Patch testing may be needed for recurrent contact dermatitis

name: Chlorpheniramine + Hydrocortisone Cream

dosage_guidelines.adults: Chlorpheniramine 4 mg every 6 hours oral; hydrocortisone 1% topically 2–3 times/day
dosage_guidelines.children: Chlorpheniramine 2 mg every 6 hours (6–12 years); 1% hydrocortisone topically
dosage_guidelines.duration: Until swelling and itch resolve (usually 2–5 days)

warnings: Seek emergency care if breathing difficulty or widespread swelling after bite — possible anaphylaxis
Apply ice pack first to reduce initial swelling
Avoid scratching — increases secondary infection risk

name: Pilocarpine (Oral)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 5 mg 3–4 times daily with meals
dosage_guidelines.duration: Long-term for Sjogren's; ongoing as needed for radiation-induced xerostomia

warnings: Avoid in narrow-angle glaucoma or uncontrolled asthma
Cholinergic side effects (sweating, tears) are dose-dependent — reduce dose if troublesome
Artificial saliva sprays are first-line for mild xerostomia

name: Chlorhexidine Mouthwash 0.2%

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10 mL; rinse for 60 seconds twice daily after brushing
dosage_guidelines.children: > 6 years: same as adults under supervision
dosage_guidelines.duration: 2–4 weeks; not for prolonged daily use

warnings: Use only for short-term — prolonged use causes permanent tooth staining
Not a substitute for proper brushing, flossing, and tongue scraping
Investigate and treat underlying cause (gum disease, decay, systemic causes)

name: Urea 25% Foot Cream

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply to affected heels and foot skin twice daily after bathing

side_effects: stinging if applied to cracked broken skin
warnings: Do not apply to open fissures with bleeding until healed
Soak feet in warm water for 10 minutes before application for best effect
Diabetic patients: seek podiatrist for cracked heels — high infection risk

name: Antibiotic Ointment (Mupirocin)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply twice daily to infected area after cleaning
dosage_guidelines.duration: 7–10 days; surgical partial nail avulsion if recurrent

warnings: Antibiotics treat infection but not the mechanical cause — nail margin must be addressed
Warm soaks (saline) twice daily to soften nail and drainage
Podiatry or surgical referral for recurrent ingrown nails

name: Arch Support Insoles + Ibuprofen

dosage_guidelines.adults: Ibuprofen 400 mg 3 times daily for pain; custom orthotic insoles as primary treatment
dosage_guidelines.children: Ibuprofen 5–10 mg/kg per dose if symptomatic; orthotics for painful flat feet
dosage_guidelines.duration: Orthotics: long-term; ibuprofen: short-term for flares (3–5 days)

warnings: Most flat feet in children are physiological — no treatment needed if painless
Custom orthotics are more effective than over-the-counter insoles for symptomatic cases
Physiotherapy for stretching plantar fascia and strengthening intrinsic foot muscles

name: Mirabegron

dosage_guidelines.adults: 25 mg once daily; increase to 50 mg if needed after 4–8 weeks

warnings: Monitor blood pressure — can raise BP significantly
Preferred over anticholinergics in elderly patients (no cognitive side effects)
Do not use in uncontrolled severe hypertension

name: Doxycycline

dosage_guidelines.adults: 100 mg twice daily

warnings: Empirical therapy should be started immediately if tick-borne illness suspected — do not wait for test results
Avoid in children under 8 and pregnant women — use amoxicillin for Lyme in these groups
Cover skin and use DEET repellent for prevention

name: Ibuprofen + Rest
symptoms_treated: neck and shoulder pain from stress

dosage_guidelines.adults: 400 mg 3 times daily with food (for somatic pain symptoms)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Short-term for physical symptoms; psychological intervention is primary

warnings: Pharmacotherapy only addresses physical symptoms — psychotherapy and lifestyle change are primary
Do not use long-term — address root cause (work overload, poor coping)
Refer for psychological support, mindfulness, and workplace adjustment

name: Artificial Tears (Preservative-Free)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1–2 drops per eye every 2–4 hours as needed

side_effects: packaging contamination risk with preserved drops
warnings: Apply 20-20-20 rule: every 20 minutes, look 20 feet away for 20 seconds
Adjust screen brightness and blue light filter settings
Comprehensive eye exam to rule out uncorrected refractive error contributing to symptoms

name: Diclofenac Gel (Topical)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2–4 g gel applied to affected area 4 times daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: 14 days for acute RSI; up to 4 weeks if needed

warnings: Do not apply to broken or inflamed skin
Wash hands after application unless hands are the treated area
Ergonomic correction and activity modification are essential for RSI recovery

name: Morphine + Ketamine (IV Analgesia)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV; sub-dissociative Ketamine 0.3 mg/kg IV
dosage_guidelines.children: Morphine 0.05–0.1 mg/kg IV; Ketamine 0.25 mg/kg IV
dosage_guidelines.duration: Titrate to pain relief; transition to oral multimodal analgesia as soon as possible

warnings: Have reversal agents ready — naloxone for morphine, diazepam for ketamine emergence reactions
Monitor oxygen saturation continuously
Ketamine is opioid-sparing — combination reduces morphine dose and respiratory risk

name: Acyclovir
symptoms_treated: blister rash on hands and feet

dosage_guidelines.adults: Not routinely needed (self-limiting); 400 mg 5 times daily if severe
dosage_guidelines.children: 20 mg/kg per dose 4 times daily if severe or immunocompromised
dosage_guidelines.duration: 5–7 days only if antivirals are used; most cases resolve in 7–10 days without treatment

warnings: HFMD is usually self-limiting — antivirals not needed in typical cases
Maintain hydration — mouth ulcers make eating difficult
Watch for rare neurological complications (viral meningitis, encephalitis) in severe cases

name: Penicillin V

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg twice daily or 250 mg 4 times daily

warnings: Full 10-day course essential — prevents rheumatic fever and glomerulonephritis
Drug of choice for Group A Strep — amoxicillin is acceptable alternative
