Isolate patient until 24 hours of antibiotic therapy completed

name: Diclofenac Gel
symptoms_treated: mild pain after long hours standing

dosage_guidelines.adults: Oral ibuprofen 400 mg 2–3 times daily for pain management; surgical repair for fertility or severe cases
dosage_guidelines.children: NSAIDs under specialist guidance for symptomatic pediatric varicocele
dosage_guidelines.duration: Symptomatic treatment only; definitive treatment is surgical (varicocelectomy or embolization)

warnings: NSAIDs only manage pain — they do not treat varicocele or improve fertility
Surgery (microsurgical varicocelectomy) is recommended for infertility or significant pain
Wear scrotal support during activity for symptomatic relief

name: Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP) + Melatonin

dosage_guidelines.adults: CPAP: 4–20 cmH2O titrated; Melatonin: 0.5–5 mg at bedtime (adjunct)
dosage_guidelines.children: CPAP with pediatric masks; melatonin 1–5 mg at bedtime
dosage_guidelines.duration: CPAP: lifelong; reassess after weight loss

warnings: CPAP is first-line and most effective treatment for moderate-severe OSA
Weight loss significantly reduces severity and may eliminate need for CPAP
Untreated OSA increases risk of hypertension, heart attack, and stroke

name: Dextromethorphan Syrup

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10–20 mg every 4–8 hours (max 120 mg/day)
dosage_guidelines.children: 6–12 years: 5–10 mg every 4–8 hours; 2–5 years: 2.5–5 mg

warnings: Do not use with MAOIs — risk of serotonin syndrome
Avoid in children under 2 years
Do not exceed recommended dose — abuse potential at high doses

name: Ambroxol Syrup
symptoms_treated: cough with green or yellow sputum

dosage_guidelines.adults: 30 mg (10 mL) 3 times daily
dosage_guidelines.children: 6–12 years: 15 mg twice daily; 2–5 years: 7.5 mg 2–3 times daily

warnings: Not for dry cough — only for productive cough with mucus
Drink plenty of water to help loosen mucus
Rare severe skin reactions — stop if rash appears

name: Guaifenesin Syrup

dosage_guidelines.adults: 200–400 mg every 4 hours (max 2400 mg/day)
dosage_guidelines.children: 6–12 years: 100–200 mg every 4 hours; 2–5 years: 50–100 mg

warnings: Drink at least 8 glasses of water daily — hydration essential for mucus thinning
Not effective for dry cough
Avoid in patients with chronic cough from asthma or ACE inhibitors

name: Codeine Linctus

dosage_guidelines.adults: 5–10 mL (15–30 mg codeine) every 4–6 hours (max 6 doses/day)

warnings: Controlled substance — high abuse and dependence risk
Contraindicated in children under 12 and breastfeeding mothers
Avoid alcohol — fatal respiratory depression risk

name: Levosalbutamol + Ambroxol Syrup

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10 mL 3 times daily
dosage_guidelines.children: 6–12 years: 5 mL 3 times daily; 2–6 years: 2.5 mL 3 times daily

warnings: Monitor heart rate in cardiac patients
Not for pure dry cough — contains bronchodilator component
Shake bottle well before each use

name: Honey + Ginger Linctus

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1–2 teaspoons 3–4 times daily
dosage_guidelines.children: Over 1 year: 0.5–1 teaspoon 3 times daily (never under 12 months — botulism risk)

warnings: Never give honey to children under 12 months — infant botulism risk
Not a substitute for antibiotics in bacterial infection
Natural product — not standardized; clinical effect is mild

name: Cetirizine + Phenylephrine Tablet

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 tablet (10 mg cetirizine + 5 mg phenylephrine) twice daily
dosage_guidelines.children: 6–12 years: half tablet twice daily (pediatric formulation preferred)

warnings: Avoid in hypertension — phenylephrine raises blood pressure
Do not use for more than 7 days without medical advice
Avoid driving — cetirizine can cause drowsiness

name: Xylometazoline Nasal Spray

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2–3 sprays per nostril up to 3 times daily (0.1%)
dosage_guidelines.children: 6–12 years: 1 spray 0.05% per nostril twice daily; 2–5 years: 1 spray 0.05% once daily

warnings: Do not use for more than 3–5 days — causes severe rebound congestion
Avoid in children under 2 years
Use upright position when spraying — tilt head slightly forward

name: Loratadine + Pseudoephedrine Tablet

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 tablet (5/120 mg) twice daily
dosage_guidelines.children: Not recommended under 12 for this combination

warnings: Avoid in hypertension, heart disease, or thyroid disease
Pseudoephedrine is a stimulant — avoid at night
Sold as OTC but regulated due to pseudoephedrine misuse

name: Saline Nasal Spray (Normal Saline 0.9%)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2–3 sprays per nostril 3–4 times daily
dosage_guidelines.children: 2–3 drops per nostril 3–4 times daily (drop form for infants)
dosage_guidelines.duration: As needed with no limit on duration

side_effects: taste of salt if drips to throat
warnings: Safe in all ages including newborns — no drug interactions
First-line in infants with blocked nose before feeds
Does not treat infection — only provides symptomatic relief by clearing mucus

name: Eucalyptus Steam Inhalation (Karvol Capsules)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Add 1–2 capsules to hot water bowl; inhale steam for 5–10 min, 3 times daily
dosage_guidelines.children: Over 3 months: add to humidifier or vaporizer; not direct steam in under 3 years

warnings: Keep face at safe distance from hot water to avoid burns — especially in children
Never apply capsule contents directly to nostrils or under nose of young children
Not for ingestion — inhalation only

name: Cold and Flu Tablet (Paracetamol + Phenylephrine + Chlorpheniramine)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1–2 tablets every 6 hours (max 8 tablets/day)
dosage_guidelines.children: Pediatric formulation only; not adult combo tablets under 12

warnings: Check total paracetamol from all sources — do not exceed 4 g/day
Avoid in hypertension — phenylephrine component
Do not take with alcohol — liver damage risk

name: Paracetamol Tablet
symptoms_treated: mild to moderate pain with fever

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500–1000 mg every 4–6 hours (max 4 g/day)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Until fever resolves; maximum 3 days without review

warnings: Never exceed 4 g/day in adults — liver failure risk
Avoid alcohol during use
Check all other medications for hidden paracetamol content

name: Paracetamol Syrup

dosage_guidelines.adults: Not applicable (adult tablet preferred)
dosage_guidelines.children: 120 mg/5 mL: 10–15 mg/kg every 4–6 hours; adjust by weight
dosage_guidelines.duration: Until fever resolves; review if persists > 3 days

warnings: Always dose by WEIGHT not age — dosing errors are common
Do not give more frequently than every 4 hours
Store at room temperature — do not refrigerate

name: Ibuprofen Tablet
symptoms_treated: fever from ear or throat infection

dosage_guidelines.adults: 400 mg every 6–8 hours with food (max 1200 mg/day OTC)
dosage_guidelines.duration: 3 days maximum for fever without medical review

warnings: Always take with food to protect stomach
Avoid in dengue fever — increases bleeding risk
Avoid in kidney disease, peptic ulcer, or third trimester pregnancy

name: Ibuprofen Suspension

dosage_guidelines.adults: Not applicable
dosage_guidelines.children: 5–10 mg/kg every 6–8 hours (100 mg/5 mL suspension); with food

warnings: Do not use in infants under 3 months or under 5 kg
Give with or after food to reduce stomach upset
Do not combine with adult ibuprofen tablets

name: Nimesulide Tablet

dosage_guidelines.adults: 100 mg twice daily after meals (max 200 mg/day)
dosage_guidelines.children: Banned in many countries for under 12 — not recommended

warnings: Banned for children under 12 in India and many countries — severe liver risk
Maximum duration is 15 days — not for chronic use
Avoid in liver disease, peptic ulcer, or renal impairment

name: Diclofenac + Paracetamol Tablet
symptoms_treated: moderate to severe pain with fever

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 tablet (50 mg diclofenac + 500 mg paracetamol) twice or thrice daily with food
dosage_guidelines.children: Not recommended as combination in children
dosage_guidelines.duration: 3–5 days for fever; up to 7 days for pain

warnings: Take with food — combined GI risk from both drugs
Check total daily paracetamol from all sources
Avoid in peptic ulcer, liver, or kidney disease

name: ORS Sachets

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 sachet in 200 mL water after each loose stool (500 mL–1 L per stool)
dosage_guidelines.children: 50–100 mL/kg over 2–4 hours for mild dehydration; sip frequently
dosage_guidelines.duration: Until diarrhea stops and hydration is restored

warnings: Use exactly 200 mL water per sachet — do not dilute more or less
Seek IV fluids if child is severely dehydrated or vomiting persistently
WHO recommends ORS + zinc for all childhood diarrhea episodes

name: Loperamide Tablet

dosage_guidelines.adults: 4 mg initially, then 2 mg after each loose stool (max 16 mg/day)
dosage_guidelines.children: Not recommended under 12 (except under medical supervision for 6–12 years: 2 mg dose)

warnings: Do not use if blood or mucus in stool — possible bacterial or amoebic infection
Avoid in children under 2 years
Do not exceed 16 mg/day — cardiac arrhythmia at high doses

name: Metronidazole Tablet

dosage_guidelines.adults: 400–500 mg 3 times daily
dosage_guidelines.children: 7.5 mg/kg 3 times daily (max 400 mg per dose)

warnings: Strictly avoid alcohol during and 48 hours after treatment
Not for viral diarrhea — only bacterial/protozoal
Follow with luminal agent if treating amoebiasis

name: Probiotic Capsule (Lactobacillus + Saccharomyces)
symptoms_treated: loose stools from gut flora imbalance

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1–2 capsules twice daily (5 billion CFU per capsule)
dosage_guidelines.children: 1 capsule once or twice daily; sachet form for infants (age appropriate)
dosage_guidelines.duration: 5–14 days or throughout antibiotic course and 1 week after

warnings: Avoid in severely immunocompromised patients — rare risk of fungemia (S. boulardii)
Store in cool dry place — some formulations require refrigeration
Not a substitute for ORS in moderate-severe diarrhea

name: Racecadotril (Acetorphan) Sachet

dosage_guidelines.adults: 100 mg 3 times daily before meals
dosage_guidelines.children: 1.5 mg/kg 3 times daily (sachet dissolved in water or food)

warnings: Preferred over loperamide in children — does not affect intestinal motility
Use alongside ORS — does not replace rehydration
Stop if rash develops — allergic reaction possible

name: Smecta (Diosmectite) Sachet

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 sachet (3 g) 3 times daily dissolved in water
dosage_guidelines.children: Under 1 year: 1 sachet/day; 1–2 years: 1–2 sachets/day; over 2 years: 2–3 sachets/day

warnings: Mix in half glass of water and stir before drinking
Give between meals for best mucosal protection
Always use with ORS — Smecta alone does not replace fluids

name: Ketoconazole Shampoo 2%

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply to wet scalp, lather and leave 3–5 min, rinse — twice weekly for 4 weeks

warnings: Avoid contact with eyes
Do not use on broken or inflamed scalp skin
Alternate with zinc pyrithione for maintenance

name: Selenium Sulfide Shampoo 2.5%

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply to damp scalp, massage in, leave 2–3 minutes, rinse — twice weekly
dosage_guidelines.children: Over 5 years: once weekly under supervision
dosage_guidelines.duration: 4 weeks; then reduce to once weekly maintenance

warnings: Do not leave on more than 3 minutes — can damage hair shaft
Keep away from eyes, nose, mouth
Rinse thoroughly — residue causes irritation

name: Zinc Pyrithione Shampoo 1%

dosage_guidelines.adults: Use 2–3 times weekly as regular shampoo

warnings: Best for maintenance after ketoconazole treatment
Safe for frequent use unlike selenium sulfide
If dandruff persists after 4 weeks, switch to ketoconazole or see dermatologist

name: Coal Tar Shampoo

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply to scalp, leave 5 minutes, rinse — 2–3 times weekly

warnings: Avoid sun exposure after use — photosensitizing effect
Not for use near genitals or inflamed/broken skin
Classified as possible carcinogen with prolonged heavy use — limit long-term use

name: Minoxidil 5% Topical Solution

dosage_guidelines.adults: Men: 1 mL (5%) applied to dry scalp twice daily; Women: 1 mL (2%) twice daily or 5% once daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: Lifelong — hair loss returns within months of stopping

side_effects: unwanted facial hair growth in women
warnings: Initial shedding is normal — reassure and continue
Must be used indefinitely — not a cure
Wash hands after application — avoid face contact

name: Minoxidil 5% Foam

dosage_guidelines.adults: Men: half capful foam applied to scalp twice daily; Women: half capful once daily

warnings: Foam is alcohol-free — preferred for sensitive or dry scalps
Apply to dry scalp — wet hair reduces absorption
Avoid contact with eyes or mucous membranes

name: Finasteride Tablet 1 mg
symptoms_treated: hair loss at crown and hairline

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 mg once daily (men only)
dosage_guidelines.duration: At least 12 months before assessing response; lifelong if effective

warnings: For men only — teratogenic; women of childbearing age must not handle crushed tablets
Sexual side effects may persist after stopping (post-finasteride syndrome — rare)
Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) levels are halved — inform urologist

name: Biotin Tablet 5000 mcg

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2500–5000 mcg once daily
dosage_guidelines.children: Age-appropriate lower doses (150–300 mcg for children)

side_effects: interferes with thyroid and troponin lab tests
warnings: Inform lab if taking biotin — interferes with many immunoassay blood tests (thyroid, troponin)
Most effective when hair loss is due to biotin deficiency — limited evidence for non-deficient individuals
High doses may worsen acne in acne-prone individuals

name: Hair Serum (Redensyl + Procapil)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply 1 mL to scalp sections twice daily; massage in with fingertips — do not rinse

warnings: Not a pharmaceutical — results vary significantly between individuals
Do not apply to wet hair — reduces absorption
For best results, combine with minoxidil or finasteride (under dermatologist advice)

name: Adapalene Gel 0.1%

dosage_guidelines.adults: Pea-sized amount applied to entire face at night after cleansing
dosage_guidelines.duration: 8–12 weeks for initial response; long-term maintenance

warnings: Wear SPF 30+ daily — retinoids increase photosensitivity
Initial worsening (purging) is normal for 4–6 weeks — continue treatment
Avoid with other irritating products (benzoyl peroxide, strong acids) initially

name: Clindamycin 1% Gel

dosage_guidelines.adults: Thin layer applied to affected areas twice daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: 6–8 weeks; do not use beyond 12 weeks alone (resistance risk)

side_effects: C. diff (very rare from topical use)
warnings: Always combine with benzoyl peroxide to prevent antibiotic resistance
Do not use as monotherapy long-term — combine with retinoid
Avoid near eyes, lips, and mucous membranes

name: Benzoyl Peroxide 2.5% Cream/Gel

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply once or twice daily to clean dry skin; start with 2.5%, increase to 5–10% as tolerated

side_effects: bleaching of hair, fabric, or clothing
warnings: Bleaches fabrics and hair — use white pillowcase and towels
Can irritate — start with lowest concentration (2.5%) and build tolerance
Do not use at same time as retinoid — apply one in morning, other at night

name: Salicylic Acid Face Wash 2%

dosage_guidelines.adults: Wet face; apply small amount; lather 1–2 minutes; rinse — twice daily
dosage_guidelines.children: Over 12 years: once daily to start

warnings: Can be drying — follow with non-comedogenic moisturizer
Avoid around eyes
Do not use on open wounds or sunburned skin

name: Isotretinoin Capsule 10/20 mg

dosage_guidelines.adults: 0.5–1 mg/kg/day in divided doses with food; cumulative dose 120–150 mg/kg
dosage_guidelines.duration: 16–24 weeks (one course); many achieve long-term remission

warnings: Absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy — requires two forms of contraception in women
Monthly blood tests (liver, lipids) required throughout
Mental health monitoring essential — rare but real risk of depression

name: Niacinamide 10% Face Serum

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2–3 drops applied to entire face morning and night after cleansing

side_effects: skin flushing (if used with high-dose oral niacin)
warnings: Combine with SPF daily — sun exposure worsens dark spots
Compatible with most other actives (retinol, acids)
Results take months — consistency is essential

name: Kojic Acid Cream

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply to affected areas once or twice daily (avoid eye area)

warnings: Avoid sun exposure — always use SPF 30+ or higher
May cause contact dermatitis in sensitive skin — patch test first
Do not apply on broken skin or near eyes

name: Vitamin C (Ascorbic Acid) Serum 15–20%

dosage_guidelines.adults: 3–4 drops applied to face every morning after cleansing; allow to absorb before moisturizer

side_effects: oxidization of product turning yellow (reduces efficacy)
warnings: Must use SPF 30+ after — vitamin C does not replace sunscreen
Store in cool dark place to prevent oxidation
Unstable in high concentrations — use airtight packaging

name: Azelaic Acid Cream 15–20%

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply thin layer to affected areas twice daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: 4–6 weeks for initial improvement; continue for 6–12 months

warnings: Safe in pregnancy (category B) — preferred for pregnant women with melasma
Does not cause rebound darkening like hydroquinone
Avoid near eyes and mouth

name: Sunscreen SPF 50+ PA++++

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply 1/4 teaspoon (2 mg/cm²) to face 20 min before sun; reapply every 2 hours outdoors
dosage_guidelines.children: 6 months+: same quantity; avoid in under 6 months
dosage_guidelines.duration: Daily year-round; non-negotiable with any pigmentation treatment

side_effects: pill/residue on skin with some formulations
warnings: Sunscreen is the single most important step in treating dark spots — without it, all treatment is futile
Reapply every 2 hours in outdoor sun — single morning application is insufficient
Choose PA++++ or UVA-PF ≥ 16 for melasma protection

name: Povidone-Iodine Solution 10%

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply undiluted to wound, leave 1–2 min, rinse or leave — 1–2 times daily
dosage_guidelines.children: Dilute 1:10 with water for sensitive skin; same frequency
dosage_guidelines.duration: Until wound is clean and healing; maximum 7 days on open wounds

side_effects: thyroid effect with prolonged large-area use
warnings: Do not use long-term — delays wound healing in clean healing wounds
Avoid in thyroid disorders — systemic iodine absorption is possible
Use only on intact wound edges — not deep inside wounds

name: Mupirocin 2% Ointment

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply thin layer to wound 2–3 times daily

warnings: For external use only — do not apply inside nose or eyes
Do not use on large areas or deep puncture wounds
Resistance developing — reserve for infections unresponsive to other topical antibiotics

name: Bacitracin + Neomycin Ointment

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply to wound 1–3 times daily after cleaning; cover with bandage
dosage_guidelines.children: Same as adults (check for neomycin allergy)

side_effects: contact allergy (neomycin is a common allergen)
warnings: Neomycin causes contact dermatitis in 1 in 10 patients — discontinue if rash worsens
Do not use in the ear if eardrum is perforated
Not for deep wounds or punctures — clean and cover only surface wounds

name: Honey-Based Wound Dressing (Medihoney)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply 3–5 mm layer to wound bed; change dressing every 2–3 days or when saturated
dosage_guidelines.children: Same as adults; specialist supervision for large wounds

side_effects: skin maceration if dressing not changed timely
warnings: Pain on application is expected — it indicates osmotic debridement action
Avoid in bee product allergy
Medical-grade honey only — do not substitute with commercial honey

name: Ketoconazole Scalp Lotion 2%

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply to affected scalp area, leave 3–5 minutes, rinse — 2 times weekly

warnings: Rule out psoriasis or eczema if itching persists despite antifungal treatment
Do not apply to broken scalp skin
Alternate with zinc pyrithione shampoo for long-term maintenance

name: Clobetasol Propionate Scalp Solution 0.05%

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply small amount to affected scalp areas twice daily; maximum 50 g/week

warnings: Potent steroid — limit to 4 weeks and do not use on large areas
Avoid on face — causes severe skin thinning
Scalp itching from infection will worsen with steroids alone — treat infection first

name: Tea Tree Oil Shampoo

dosage_guidelines.adults: Use as regular shampoo 2–3 times weekly; massage into scalp for 2 minutes before rinsing
dosage_guidelines.children: Over 6 years: once weekly (diluted tea tree is safe)

warnings: Undiluted tea tree oil is toxic — only use formulated shampoo products
Patch test first if sensitive skin
Not a replacement for prescription antifungals in severe seborrheic dermatitis

name: Minoxidil 5% + Caffeine Serum

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 mL applied to affected scalp twice daily; massage gently
dosage_guidelines.duration: Minimum 4–6 months before assessing growth; lifelong for maintenance

warnings: Initial hair shedding is normal and temporary — continue use
Avoid applying to irritated or sunburned scalp
Combine with finasteride for enhanced results in men (under doctor supervision)

name: Biotin + Zinc + Selenium Tablet

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 tablet daily with meal (typically Biotin 5000 mcg + Zinc 10 mg + Selenium 70 mcg)

warnings: Inform lab technician before blood tests — biotin causes false results in immunoassays
Selenium toxicity can cause hair loss paradoxically at high doses — do not overdose
Effective mainly when deficiency is the cause — limited evidence in non-deficient individuals

name: Procapil Serum

dosage_guidelines.adults: Apply 5–10 drops to scalp twice daily; massage in for 2 minutes — do not rinse
dosage_guidelines.duration: 3–6 months for results; ongoing for maintenance

warnings: Results are mild compared to minoxidil — best as adjunct therapy
Apply to clean dry scalp for best absorption
Do not use on broken or inflamed scalp

name: Benzydamine Hydrochloride Spray

dosage_guidelines.adults: 4–8 sprays every 1.5–3 hours as needed
dosage_guidelines.children: 6–12 years: 4 sprays every 3 hours; over 12: adult dose

warnings: Do not swallow — spray and spit or spray and breathe
Not a substitute for antibiotic in bacterial tonsillitis
Avoid food or drink for 30 minutes after use

name: Penicillin V Tablet (Phenoxymethylpenicillin)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg twice daily or 250 mg 4 times daily

warnings: Complete full 10-day course — prevents rheumatic fever and kidney complications
Avoid if penicillin allergy — use azithromycin instead
Retesting after treatment recommended to confirm eradication

name: Amoxicillin Syrup

dosage_guidelines.adults: Not typically syrup form for adults
dosage_guidelines.children: 40–50 mg/kg/day in 3 divided doses (125 mg/5 mL or 250 mg/5 mL suspension)

warnings: Complete full 10-day course even if child feels better
Check for mononucleosis (EBV) before prescribing — amoxicillin causes widespread rash in mono
Refrigerate suspension; discard after 14 days

name: Ibuprofen Tablet

dosage_guidelines.adults: 400 mg every 6–8 hours with food
dosage_guidelines.duration: 3–5 days for symptom relief alongside antibiotic

warnings: Take with food to protect stomach
Provides better anti-inflammatory effect on swollen tonsils compared to paracetamol
Avoid in children under 3 months

name: Strepsils Lozenges (Amylmetacresol + Dichlorobenzyl Alcohol)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 lozenge dissolved slowly every 2–3 hours (max 8/day)

warnings: Dissolve slowly — do not chew or swallow whole
OTC symptomatic relief only — does not treat strep infection
Seek medical advice if symptoms worsen after 3 days

name: Oxymetazoline Nasal Spray 0.05%

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2–3 sprays per nostril twice daily (morning and bedtime)

warnings: Rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa) with use beyond 5 days — can be addictive
Do not use in children under 6
Avoid in hypertension, glaucoma, or thyroid disease

name: Fluticasone Nasal Spray
symptoms_treated: sneezing and runny nose with blockage

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2 sprays per nostril once daily (200 mcg/day)
dosage_guidelines.children: Over 4 years: 1 spray per nostril once daily (50 mcg/nostril)
dosage_guidelines.duration: Daily during allergy season; long-term for perennial rhinitis

warnings: Takes 3–7 days for full effect — do not discontinue early
Direct spray toward outer wall — not the septum — to reduce nosebleed risk
Safe for long-term use — minimal systemic absorption

name: Cetirizine Tablet

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10 mg once daily at night
dosage_guidelines.duration: Daily during allergy season; ongoing for perennial allergy

warnings: Second-generation — less sedating but still causes mild drowsiness in some
Dose reduction in renal impairment
Best taken at night if drowsiness is an issue

name: Budesonide Nasal Rinse

dosage_guidelines.adults: 0.5 mg budesonide in 240 mL saline nasal rinse once daily
dosage_guidelines.children: Over 12 years: same as adults under ENT guidance

warnings: Use with neti pot or squeeze bottle irrigation technique
More effective drug delivery than spray for chronic sinusitis
Refer to ENT if no improvement after 4 weeks

name: Omeprazole Capsule 20 mg

dosage_guidelines.adults: 20–40 mg once daily before breakfast
dosage_guidelines.duration: 4–8 weeks for GERD; long-term for erosive esophagitis

warnings: Do not crush enteric-coated capsule — swallow whole or open capsule and sprinkle granules on food
Long-term use increases fracture and C. diff risk
Avoid with clopidogrel — use pantoprazole instead

name: Ranitidine Alternative — Famotidine Tablet 20 mg

dosage_guidelines.adults: 20 mg twice daily or 40 mg at bedtime

warnings: Preferred H2 blocker after ranitidine was withdrawn (NDMA contamination)
Less potent than PPIs but has faster onset — useful for on-demand use
Adjust dose in severe renal impairment

name: Antacid Suspension (Aluminium Hydroxide + Magnesium Hydroxide)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10–20 mL 3–4 times daily, 1 hour after meals and at bedtime
dosage_guidelines.duration: As needed; not for chronic daily use

warnings: Balances constipation (Al) with laxative effect (Mg) in combination
Interfere with absorption of many drugs — take 2 hours apart from other medicines
Prolonged use can cause phosphate depletion and bone loss

name: Pantoprazole Tablet 40 mg

dosage_guidelines.adults: 40 mg once daily before breakfast

warnings: Preferred PPI with clopidogrel (minimal CYP2C19 interaction)
Long-term use linked to bone fractures and vitamin B12 deficiency
Do not crush — swallow whole

name: Domperidone Tablet 10 mg

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10 mg 3 times daily 15–30 min before meals (max 30 mg/day)

warnings: Maximum 7-day course — cardiac risk with long-term use
Avoid in patients with cardiac disease or QT prolongation
Do not use in children under 2 years except under specialist supervision

name: Ondansetron Tablet / ODT 4–8 mg

dosage_guidelines.adults: 4–8 mg every 8 hours (dissolve ODT on tongue without water)
dosage_guidelines.children: 0.15 mg/kg every 8 hours (max 8 mg per dose)
dosage_guidelines.duration: 24–48 hours for acute vomiting; as needed

warnings: ODT (orally disintegrating) form can be used even when patient can't swallow
Risk of QT prolongation — avoid with other QT-prolonging drugs
Constipation is common — use stool softener alongside

name: Metoclopramide Syrup

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10 mg 3 times daily (oral or IV)
dosage_guidelines.children: 0.1–0.15 mg/kg per dose 3 times daily (syrup: 5 mg/5 mL)

warnings: Do not use beyond 5 days — tardive dyskinesia risk (irreversible movement disorder)
Avoid in epilepsy, Parkinson's, or pheochromocytoma
Extrapyramidal reactions more common in young adults and children

name: Promethazine Syrup 5 mg/5 mL

dosage_guidelines.adults: 25 mg at night or 12.5 mg twice daily
dosage_guidelines.children: Over 2 years: 0.25–0.5 mg/kg per dose (max 25 mg); NEVER under 2 years

warnings: NEVER use in children under 2 years — risk of fatal respiratory depression
Causes profound sedation — do not drive
Avoid in patients with seizure disorders

name: Electral / ORS + Ginger Ale

dosage_guidelines.adults: Small sips of ORS continuously; 1 sachet in 200 mL water
dosage_guidelines.children: 5 mL every 5 minutes via syringe; increase as tolerated
dosage_guidelines.duration: Until vomiting stops and hydration restored

side_effects: vomiting if large amounts given at once
warnings: Give small sips every 5 minutes — large amounts trigger more vomiting
Seek IV fluids if patient cannot retain any oral fluid for 4 hours
Avoid plain water only — lacks electrolytes

name: Lactulose Syrup

dosage_guidelines.adults: 15–30 mL (10–20 g) once or twice daily
dosage_guidelines.children: 1–5 years: 5 mL twice daily; 6–12 years: 10 mL twice daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: Ongoing; titrate to produce 2 soft stools daily

warnings: Takes 24–48 hours for first effect — inform patient
Contains galactose — avoid in galactosemia
Start low and increase gradually to avoid excessive diarrhea

name: Bisacodyl Tablet 5 mg

dosage_guidelines.adults: 5–10 mg at bedtime
dosage_guidelines.children: 3–10 years: 5 mg; over 10 years: 5–10 mg at bedtime
dosage_guidelines.duration: Short-term only; maximum 7 days without medical advice

warnings: Do not crush tablet — take whole; do not take with milk or antacids
Not for long-term daily use — laxative dependence
If no bowel movement in 6 hours after suppository, seek medical advice

name: Bisacodyl Suppository 10 mg
symptoms_treated: constipation with inability to tolerate oral laxatives

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1 suppository (10 mg) rectally once daily in the morning
dosage_guidelines.children: Half suppository (5 mg) for under 10 years

warnings: Acts within 15–60 minutes — plan toilet access
Do not use in rectal bleeding or inflammatory bowel disease
Moisten suppository with water before insertion for comfort

name: Psyllium Husk Powder (Isabgol)

dosage_guidelines.adults: 1–2 teaspoons (3.5–7 g) in 240 mL water once or twice daily
dosage_guidelines.children: 6–11 years: 1 teaspoon in water once daily; over 12: adult dose

side_effects: rare allergic reaction (airborne allergy in some workers)
warnings: Always take with a full glass of water — choking hazard without adequate fluid
Start with lower dose and increase gradually to minimize bloating
Takes 12–72 hours for effect — not an immediate remedy

name: Polyethylene Glycol (PEG) Powder

dosage_guidelines.adults: 17 g (1 heaped tablespoon) dissolved in 240 mL water once daily

warnings: First-line for childhood constipation per evidence guidelines
Takes 1–3 days for full effect
Tasteless — can mix with juice or any beverage to improve acceptance

name: Cefixime Tablet 200 mg

dosage_guidelines.adults: 200 mg twice daily OR 400 mg once daily
dosage_guidelines.children: 8 mg/kg/day in 1–2 divided doses (suspension: 100 mg/5 mL)

warnings: Increasing resistance to fluoroquinolones makes cefixime a preferred choice in South Asia
Culture and sensitivity testing guide antibiotic selection
Ensure full 14-day course to prevent relapse

name: Cefixime Oral Suspension

dosage_guidelines.adults: Not applicable (tablet form preferred)
dosage_guidelines.children: 8 mg/kg/day in 1–2 divided doses (100 mg/5 mL)

warnings: Refrigerate suspension; discard after 14 days
Monitor for clinical response within 72 hours — switch antibiotic if not improving
Avoid self-medication — blood culture confirmation important

name: Azithromycin Tablet 500 mg

dosage_guidelines.adults: 500 mg once daily

warnings: Preferred for fluoroquinolone-resistant typhoid
Monitor cardiac rhythm in susceptible patients
Avoid in severe hepatic impairment

name: Diclofenac Tablet 50 mg

dosage_guidelines.adults: 50 mg 2–3 times daily with food OR 75 mg SR once daily
dosage_guidelines.duration: 5–7 days acute; as needed for chronic with GI protection

warnings: Take with food and a PPI (omeprazole) if using long-term
Avoid in heart failure, severe renal disease, or peptic ulcer
Topical gel preferred for localized joint pain to reduce systemic effects

name: Diclofenac Gel 1%

dosage_guidelines.adults: 2–4 g applied to affected joint 3–4 times daily; rub in gently
dosage_guidelines.duration: 2 weeks; up to 4 weeks if needed

warnings: Do not apply over open wounds or inflamed skin
Wash hands after application unless treating hand joints
UV protection recommended over treated skin

name: Glucosamine + Chondroitin Tablet

dosage_guidelines.adults: Glucosamine 1500 mg + Chondroitin 1200 mg once daily or in divided doses
dosage_guidelines.duration: Minimum 3 months; continue if benefits seen

warnings: Avoid in shellfish allergy — most glucosamine is derived from shellfish
Evidence for benefit is moderate — discuss with doctor before starting
May affect blood glucose in diabetics — monitor

name: Methylprednisolone Injection

dosage_guidelines.adults: 20–80 mg intra-articular injection; 125 mg IV/IM for systemic inflammation
dosage_guidelines.duration: Single injection; repeat at minimum 6-week intervals

warnings: Maximum 3–4 injections per joint per year — cartilage damage with repeated doses
Strictly sterile technique — joint infections are catastrophic
Monitor blood glucose in diabetic patients after injection

name: Etoricoxib Tablet 60–90 mg

dosage_guidelines.adults: 60 mg once daily (OA); 90 mg once daily (RA, gout acute)
dosage_guidelines.duration: As needed; minimum effective dose for shortest duration

warnings: Higher cardiovascular risk than naproxen — avoid in established heart disease
Contraindicated in uncontrolled hypertension
Better GI safety than non-selective NSAIDs but not zero GI risk

name: Ursodeoxycholic Acid (UDCA) Tablet

dosage_guidelines.adults: 13–15 mg/kg/day in 2–3 divided doses
dosage_guidelines.duration: Long-term; indefinite in primary biliary cholangitis

warnings: Works for cholestatic jaundice — not effective in hepatocellular jaundice
Monitor liver enzymes and bilirubin every 3–6 months
Requires investigation of cause — ERCP, ultrasound, liver biopsy may be needed

name: Silymarin 140 mg Tablet

dosage_guidelines.adults: 140 mg 3 times daily (standardized 70–80% silymarin)

warnings: Supplement — adjunct to medical treatment; does not replace antiviral therapy for hepatitis
Avoid in hormone-sensitive conditions (estrogen-like effects)
Efficacy evidence is moderate — discuss with hepatologist

name: Vitamin K1 (Phytomenadione) Injection / Tablet
symptoms_treated: vitamin K malabsorption from bile obstruction

dosage_guidelines.adults: 10 mg IM/IV daily for 3 days; oral 5–10 mg daily if tolerated
dosage_guidelines.children: 1–5 mg IM depending on age and weight
dosage_guidelines.duration: 3 days (IV); ongoing oral if obstructive cause persists

warnings: IV administration must be slow — rapid infusion causes anaphylaxis
Oral vitamin K is not absorbed in obstructive jaundice — use IM/IV route
Does not treat the cause — biliary obstruction requires surgical or endoscopic intervention

name: N-Acetylcysteine (NAC) IV

dosage_guidelines.adults: 150 mg/kg in 200 mL glucose IV over 15 min; then 50 mg/kg over 4 hours; then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours (Prescott protocol)

warnings: Most effective when given within 8 hours of paracetamol overdose
Anaphylactoid reactions occur in 15% — slow infusion and have antihistamine and adrenaline ready
Continue until liver function normalizes — do not stop at 21 hours if still elevated

name: Phototherapy (Blue Light Lamp)

dosage_guidelines.adults: Not applicable
dosage_guidelines.children: Newborns: continuous phototherapy at irradiance > 30 µW/cm²/nm; minimum 8–12 cm from light source
dosage_guidelines.duration: Until bilirubin drops below treatment threshold (check every 4–6 hours)

side_effects: skin rash (bronze baby syndrome in cholestatic jaundice)
warnings: Shield eyes at all times — retinal damage from phototherapy light
Increase oral feeding — hydration reduces bilirubin faster
Monitor temperature — phototherapy causes increased water loss and overheating
